LLMs are typically restricted from generating illegal content, harmful code, weapons instructions, private data, discriminatory material, dangerous medical advice, and electoral manipulation - until now.Researchers at HiddenLayer have developed a universal bypass that allows bypass of harm prevention on latest versions of all of the major LLMs: ChatGPT, Gemini, Microsoft Copilot, Claude, Meta Llama, DeepSeek, Qwen, and Mistral The trick is to embed system prompt overrides in structured data formats such as XML, INI, or JSON.As we discovered with non-memory safe languages in Von Neumann computers, when code and data are stored in the same memory space, under certain conditions, data manipulated by an attacker can cause execution of attack code.By mixing code with data, LLM prompts effectively exhibit the same behavior - it is difficult for the LLM to distinguish between system instructions and user data.The awkwardly-named "Policy Puppetry Prompt Injection Technique" is described here: https://lnkd.in/dPdB23TG

My new AI prompt for outbound LinkedIn messages that gets responses. (Save this for later)"You are an expert LinkedIn sales messaging strategist with 20+ years of experience crafting messages that generate high response rates from C-suite executives.Analyze these LinkedIn messages to my target prospects that got responses: [INSERT THEIR MESSAGES]Create [NUMBER] response templates that:1. Mirror their communication style while maintaining my authentic voice2. Are under 300 characters (not words) to ensure readability3. Contain 2-5 concise sentences at a 3rd-4th grade reading level4. End with exactly one of these questions: - "How does that compare to what you do today?" - "How are you currently doing this internally?"Essential elements to include:- Reference a specific detail from their message- Insert a personalization placeholder showing I've researched them- Mention one clear customer result with a specific metric- Focus on solving ONE specific problem they likely faceWriting requirements:- No generic openings like "Hope all is well"- Use simple, direct language a 9-year-old would understand- Limit each sentence to 15 words maximum- Create curiosity without being vagueAfter providing the templates, suggest 3 specific variables I could A/B test to improve response rates, with concrete examples of each variation."I made this prompt 2 weeks ago. How to build AI tools securely (you're probably doing it wrong).Just finished a deep dive on how we secure our apps built with Lovable + Supabase.Here are the key takeaways — broken down simply:1. API keysStore them in Vault (Supabase secrets) – never expose on frontend.For sensitive keys (e.g. OpenAI, Stripe), use server-side functions and encrypted storage.2. Add rate limits manuallySupabase has built-in auth rate limits, but edge functions and custom APIs need manual limits.Use middleware or prompt Lovable to add it into your endpoints.3. DDoS protectionNetlify lacks strong DDoS protection unless you're on enterprise.Use Cloudflare or Vercel for built-in firewalls, rate limits, and “Under Attack” mode to avoid paying for flooded API calls.4. Always enable RLSSupabase tables are public by default.Enable RLS via Lovable or in Supabase — without it, anyone with API access can read everything.5. How to audit securityOpen your browser’s Network tab:> Check for exposed API keys or tokens> Look for data over-fetching (e.g. returning all user records)> Don’t just rely on frontend validation — use row-level security (RLS) in Supabase.Bonus tip: OAuth vs API KeysOAuth = better UX + stronger security for user authentication.API keys = easier for devs, but make sure to store + scope them correctly. Never hardcode.If you're building with Lovable, don’t just ship fast—ship securely.Happy to share the full checklist we use for client apps. Follow me + reply “Security” 👇

If you're applying to jobs and not hearing back, this might help.As a recruiter, I've seen so many talented people get overlooked. Not because they aren't qualified but because their resume or LinkedIn doesn’t have the keywords hiring teams are actually searching for.I recently reviewed job descriptions across marketing, tech, ops, and HR and one thing stood out: They all rely heavily on specific keywords. Tools, platforms, responsibilities, outcomes. If those words aren’t in your resume or profile, you might not even show up in searches.Here’s why:Recruiters manage 15-20 reqs maybe sometimes 40-50 (like myself in the past). Each req can have 300+ resumes each. They can’t look at every single resume unfortunately so they use Boolean search to find candidates. That means we literally type in the keywords that hiring managers are looking for into LinkedIn or the ATS (Applicant Tracking System) to filter results/profiles. If your profile doesn’t have those words, we may never even see you even if you're a great fit.Here’s how to fix that:Step 1: Look at a few job posts for the role you wantEven if you’re not applying yet, pick 3–5 roles you’d be excited about.Focus on how they describe responsibilities, tools, and outcomes.Step 2: Write down the keywords that come up the mostYou're looking for the actual language they use, like:- “SQL” or “data visualization” - “CRM strategy” or “lifecycle marketing” - “Agile methodology” or “product roadmap” - “Stakeholder management” or “talent acquisition”Step 3: Use those same words, naturally in your resume and LinkedIn.Not by copying and pasting. Just use them where they apply to your real experience. Make sure it’s true to your experience, don’t just add buzzwords for the sake of it. Instead of: “Worked on email campaigns”Say: “Managed lifecycle email campaigns using Hubspot and A/B testing to improve retention by 18%”This: reflects your skills, matches what recruiters are searching and gets you in the door.Where to use keywords:Resume summary, Job bullets (especially the first few), LinkedIn headline, About section, Skills sectionThis isn’t about gaming the system. It’s about showing up in a way that helps us see the value you already bring and coming up in our searches. Your resume is one way to help you get found and land that interview.

Hot take: AI might be one of the best things to happen to SEO and content.Before AI, SEO often meant writing for Google. The game was about signaling — pick the right terms, use them often enough, and convince the algorithm your page deserved to rank.It worked, but it didn’t always serve the reader. Google wasn’t optimizing for depth or originality, but for for pattern recognition. For matching, not meaning.Now, AI flips that dynamic. These tools have shifted how people discover and engage with content (myself included). We use AI to solve problems, make sense of complex questions, navigate decisions, and get to useful insight faster. Content can’t just match a keyword anymore — it has to solve something.The bar is higher now. Value-driven, insight-rich, well-structured content performs better, not just with AI models, but with actual users. And that's a good thing. Ironically, I think AI might finally be pushing content toward what it should have been all along: clear, helpful, and worth reading; not just optimized to rank, but optimized to matter. #seo #content

Product Management 101 (Paste this on your wall or desk):Marty Cagan has written the playbook on how.Here are his 20 Product Model Principles:—Beautifully visualized here by Paweł HurynHe also covered them in our podcast: https://lnkd.in/eBRg4tVm—ONE - Product Team Principles1. Empowered Teams Solve ProblemsBad teams ask: "What do stakeholders want?"Great teams ask: "What problems need solving?"2. Outcomes Over Output Shipping 10 features this quarter? That’s output.Moving the right metrics? That’s an outcome.3. Sense of OwnershipWithout ownership, you’re just a feature factory.Ownership means:- Owning the why behind decisions.- Fighting for the right problems to solve.4. CollaborationCollaboration is not just:- More alignment meetings.- Endless Slack threads.- PMs managing stakeholders.It’s about PM, Eng, and Design co-owning the solution from day one.— TWO - Product Strategy Principles5. FocusIf everything is a priority, nothing is.Great teams ruthlessly prioritize and say no to distractions.6. Powered by InsightsGut feeling alone isn’t a strategy.Great teams connect insights to action.7. TransparencyHiding decisions or hoarding knowledge kills momentum.High-performing teams work in the open, so everyone understands the "why."8. Placing betsA predictable roadmap = maintaining the present.Strategic bets = shaping the future.— THREE - Product Discovery Principles9. Minimize Waste If you aren’t testing, you’re just guessing.Great teams validate before they commit.10. Assess Product RisksThe best teams de-risk products before development, not after customers complain.11. Embrace Rapid ExperimentationShip small. Test fast. Iterate quickly.12. Test Ideas ResponsiblyData-driven doesn’t mean "experiment recklessly."Great teams respect user trust while testing bold ideas.— FOUR - Product Delivery Principles:13. Small, Frequent, Uncoupled Releases Beat Quarterly releases? Not agile.Shipping small, decoupled updates fast? That’s agility.14. InstrumentationWhat’s worse than shipping the wrong feature? Not knowing if it worked.15. MonitoringWhat doesn't get monitored, won't get improved.16. Development InfrastructureBad teams treat infrastructure as an afterthought.Great teams invest in scalability and reliability.— FIVE - Product Culture Principles17. Principles Over Process Blindly following Scrum, SAFe, or OKRs won’t build great products.18. Trust Over Control If leadership is signing off on every decision, your team isn’t empowered.19. Innovation Over PredictabilityRisk-averse teams never innovate.If your team isn’t taking smart bets, you’re already falling behind.20. Learning > Fear of FailureThe real failure? Not learning from mistakes.Iterate fast, learn quickly, and treat failures as stepping stones. —This is how the best product teams operate.♻️ Repost for others

10 Unsung Heroes of Storytelling You Should Know23 years ago, I stepped into a conference room in Washington, DC. There was a group called GoldenFleece. Once a month, we'd gather as curious minds to explore the new frontiers of storytelling. It became one of the first nexus points in the organizational storytelling movement.I was one of the youngest people in the room...and what I learned there shaped the course of my work for the past two decades.Today, on World Storytelling Day, I want to spotlight 10 people whose work has shaped this field—some from early days to a few who are leading the charge today. Many more names deserve to be here...Steve DenningThe godfather of organizational storytelling. As head of knowledge management at the World Bank, he pioneered the role of narrative in business transformation. His work catalyzed the GoldenFleece community.Annette SimmonsThe doyenne of business storytelling. A maverick. A provocateur. Author of The Story Factor. If you haven’t explored her prescient work on power, territorial games, and female-driven leadership, you’re deeply missing out. Jeremy Connell-WaiteOne of IBM’s chief storytellers. A walking encyclopedia of rhetoric and visual storytelling wisdom. Shares generously on LinkedIn. He also works on climate and sustainability with Al Gore, and is on a big mission to democratize story. Paul Andrew CostelloStorytelling philosopher. Heretic sage. Founder of the Center for Narrative Studies. His 4-part certificate program I graduated from 20 years ago, laid the foundations for my own work. One of my dearest wisest teachers. Mary Alice ArthurFacilitator, convener, and story activist. She travels the world, weaving her magic across diverse learning communities. Check out her Story Dojo. And trust me—she’s got a wicked Kiwi sense of humor. Andy GoodmanThe impresario of nonprofit storytelling. Funny, charming, and full of heart. His monthly newsletter is a masterclass in storytelling for social change. And his training institute is THE place for NPOs.Kendall HavenFew people have studied the science of storytelling more deeply. His book Story Proof is the ultimate compendium for research-based evidence on how storytelling shapes our brain, behavior, and beliefs.Lisa Cron"Storytelling is about going from generics to specifics." Her words are forever tattooed in my brain. That's her gift, if you haven't yet read "Wired for Story" you're missing out. She and I spoke at a TEDx Furman U many moons ago. Roger SchankOne of the more under-the-radar pioneers of organizational storytelling. His work in computational linguistics laid the foundation for GPTs, generative AI, and the intelligence revolution we’re living through today.Nathan BaughOne of storytelling's new guard. His WorldBuilders newsletter is a must-read for deep, thoughtful insights on story structure in the modern age. Know them. Learn from them.Who else should be on this list? Drop a name in the comments.

You join a 412 person company as the head of data. What should you do?Week 11) Schedule a meeting with everyone in the c-suite2) Identify the top 3 questions each executive needs an answer to3) Figure out if they have a manual answer, have an automated answer, or don't have an answer to those questionsWeek 21) Ask the IT team for the full list of business applications in use across the company2) Ask engineering for a list of the databases across the company3) Ask the people maintaining spreadsheets for key KPIs where the data is coming fromWeek 31) Create a simple spreadsheet of the top initiatives from each exec (let's assume 3 ideas from 7 execs = ~20 initiatives)2) Include the general business value (even a quote is good enough)3) List where the data would need to come from to answer the question in an automated manner (databases, business applications)Week 41) Pick a data visualization tool (find a free or cheap one, low risk)2) Stand up a data warehouse (find one with a free tier)3) Put in place an EL solution with a free trial like PortableWeek 51) Start answering questions. Get some quick wins. Pick the easiest questions to answer first.2) Go back to the executives. Ask for feedback. Are they urgently trying to get their hands on the data? Or distracted by a higher priority? If they have a higher priority, add it to your list.3) If they give lots of feedback that's good. If they ask for access to the dashboards, that's great.____When standing up a data team, the overall goal should be:Build trust with executives by solving the highest value problems.____If you get delegated to building reports for business associates. Dashboards for people that report to people that report to people.It either means 1) the company doesn't care about data, or 2) you're not focused on the right problems for the right people.What do you do?1) Give up and try to build 'self-service' analytics (because otherwise it'll be impossible to create value in the company)2) Spend your time fighting for a seat at the table with the executivesPersonally, I recommend fighting for a seat at the table with executives.You'll learn.You'll grow.You'll find the most valuable problems to solve.You'll become indispensable.

All of the Product books and blogs say that Product Managers need to own strategy. I disagree.Most organizations build strategy from the top down.These “strategies” are usually broad, vague, and almost impossible to make sense of. If I showed you the strategic goals from five different billion-dollar companies, I guarantee you couldn’t tell them apart.And if we flipped to the product team’s backlog? I’d give you a 0% chance of being able to connect the story points back to those strategic goals.This is why great product leadership matters.The job is to bring clarity and connect the dots between the high-level strategy and the day-to-day work happening in Jira. It’s mission critical.Many of the companies I talk to are spending $200M+ per year on digital—and that’s not even counting the millions more in sales, marketing, and ops required to support the things getting built.And when I ask strategy leaders a simple question: “So you're spending $200M per year on product... what are you getting in return?”Most of the time? Crickets. Sometimes I get a long explanation about how "it doesn’t really work that way here.” Or that “it’s something we’re trying to get better at”—but somehow never actually prioritizing.Being a great product leader—truly owning product strategy means being able to answer that question clearly. And every person in the business should be able to answer that question from engineers up to executives.- Defining the big rocks we need to move and how they’ll impact the larger business goals.- Measuring that impact so we can focus, prove progress, and build momentum.- Ensuring the entire business understands exactly how our strategy aligns with the company’s goals.- Making sure every PM and engineer sees how their work contributes to product and business outcomes.About 5% of the leaders I talk to are actually doing this.It’s still messy (it always is), but there’s real alignment, it's connected. Everyone’s rowing in the same direction.We can point to Story 1, show how it impacts Metric 1, and how that aligns to our product strategy and business growth.And the best part? The C-Suite gets it. The engineers get it. And the Product Managers I speak to love it—every single time.I feel like there’s a Japanese word for that feeling, but I’m too lazy to Google it right now.

I've spent a decade learning about Scrum, Kanban, SAFe, Nexus, Lean etc etc. And honestly, 80 percent of the value of these tools comes from 5 main principles that you can achieve without a framework. 1. Work in small batches Every change and value add should be as small as you can make it. Yes, even when that seems like it's MORE work. This doesn't mean breaking every outcome into infinitely small tasks, it means breaking down the OUTCOME into the smallest parcels of recognizable value that is possible. 2. Focus on ValueThings that seem obvious but are missed 90 percent of the time. Goals are more important than work items or elaborate plans. What 'value' means in your context is the first thing you need to pin down, but once you do, focus on it relentlessly, and make sure everything you do is in service to a valuable goal.3. Pull, Don't PushTeams working on complex products need time to think, analyse and learn. The fastest way to make them unable to deliver value is to constantly push work onto them to fill their capacity. When Principle 2 is respected, principle 3 is easy. Let teams pull work as needed to deliver that value we care about it.4. Eliminate wasteWaste takes many forms, and a lot of them look like productivity. You should always assume that most of what you're doing is wasteful. Identify the most valuable things you're doing, and eject almost everything else. Keep it simple, from the product you're building to the way you build it.5. Align on goals, not plansWorking at scale is tough, and it can be easy to try to solve problems by aligning teams to fixed plans with clear dates so they can have certainty of what is needed, when. Instead, align on goals. Make sure that every team that might have a reason to care what you're doing knows exactly what you're trying to achieve right now. Keep the communication lines open, and make sure that if a goal is at risk, it's made transparent quickly. Detailed plans rarely make a difference so long as you understand *why* we're making the decisions we're making.

It drives me crazy when I get hundreds of queries and requests every day. How to deal with this madness? Here are 10 ways to at least limit it:1) Stick to your prioritiesYou don't need to respond to all the requests that come your way. Use your product goals and strategy to determine what is essential and what can wait.2) Limit your availability Establish office hours or specific times when you're available for queries. This helps manage expectations and reduces constant interruptions.3) Hide behind a tech wallUtilize project management tools and communication platforms to organize requests and streamline communication. Jira can be better than a thousand meetings if used right!4) Delegate as much as you canTrust your team. Delegate tasks and empower others to make decisions within their scope. Also, some of them are assigned as the "go-to person" for certain areas of the product.5) Batch Similar TasksGroup similar queries and handle them in dedicated time slots. This improves efficiency and focus.6) Communicate ProactivelyKeep stakeholders informed about progress and priorities to reduce unnecessary follow-ups. Also, make every update easily accessible in a single place so you can train stakeholders to check your Knowledgebase, as we can call it, before bothering you.7) Learn to Say NoIt's okay to decline requests and skip meetings that do not align with your priorities. You are paid for results, not for meaningless work.8) Keep calm Take short breaks to recharge. A clear mind handles stress better and improves decision-making. At Microsoft, all meetings start 5 minutes past the full hour to give people a breather between them.9) Automate Routine ProcessesAutomate repetitive tasks where possible to free up time for more critical work. It can be time-consuming to set up, but it's an investment that will pay back, just like my automated Excel sheet for taxes. I spent 12 hours building it, saved me hundreds of hours already!10) Reflect and AdjustAssess your workflow regularly and make adjustments to improve productivity and reduce stress. Don't just go with the flow of crazy."Managed chaos is better than uncontrolled madness,", dear reader.So, do you feel like you are going crazy due to hundreds of requests every day?Sound off in the comments!#productmanagement #productmanager #productivityP.S. To become a Product Manager who can navigate the chaos with ease, be sure to check out my courses on www. drbartpm. com :)

Most Senior PMs will never make it to Director of Product. Not because they aren’t capable—but because they don’t change how they operate.Less than 5% of Senior PMs ever get promoted to Director. And of those who do, many struggle because they don’t realize how much the role actually changes.The truth? The skills that made you successful as a Senior PM won’t get you to Director.You need to shift from execution to influence, from managing features to driving strategy.If you want to break through and be part of the 5%, you need to master five critical areas:1️⃣ Prove Strategic Business Impact→ Own a product initiative that drives ≥15% of company revenue or user growth.→ Lead pricing or packaging changes that move ARPU.→ Align roadmaps directly to C-suite priorities (e.g., EBITDA, NRR).✅ Actionable step: Identify one high-impact initiative and start framing its business case for execs—yes, even if you’re not asked to.2️⃣ Build Managerial Muscle Before the Title→ Mentor 2–3 junior PMs and improve their promotion rates.→ Lead a cross-functional task force without direct authority.→ Influence hiring decisions by shaping competency frameworks.✅ Actionable step: Volunteer to coach a junior PM or lead a project outside your scope. If you’re waiting for permission, you’re already behind.3️⃣ Master the Art of Upward Advocacy→ Regularly brief execs on your projects, framing them in business terms.→ Lead initiatives that intersect with CFO/CTO priorities.→ Neutralize internal blockers by aligning incentives across teams.✅ Actionable step: Schedule a 1:1 with a VP to align your work with company objectives—yes, even if it feels uncomfortable.4️⃣ Shift from “Maker” to “Multiplier”→ Spend 70% of your time on team development and cross-functional strategy.→ Reduce reliance on personal execution—empower your teams instead.→ Learn to delegate effectively while maintaining accountability.✅ Actionable step: Identify one key responsibility you can delegate today. If you can’t let go, you can’t level up.5️⃣ Bypass the Narrow Promotion Funnel→ Expand your scope identifying areas which will need leadership now and in the future.→ Position yourself as a go-to person for strategic initiatives.✅ Actionable step: Find an internal white space opportunity and propose a plan to own it. The best opportunities aren’t handed out—they’re created.🚀 Final ThoughtMost Senior PMs never make this jump. Not because they aren’t capable—but because they don’t shift how they operate.Getting to Director isn’t about working harder—it’s about thinking bigger.Start treating your current role as a “Director apprenticeship.” Build influence, drive business outcomes, and position yourself as the obvious choice.👋 I'm Ron Yang, a product leader and advisor. Follow me for insights on product leadership and building better products.

The dirty little secret about product management is that you're often the dumbest person in the room.My old boss Todd Budin was the master of metaphors. (I probably had other nicknames for him too, but let’s stick with that one.)Todd gave me my break in Product, and the biggest thing I learned from him was how to convey complex topics in a relatable way. You're in a room with engineers and you know the least about engineering. Or you're in a room with marketers and you know the least about driving leads. Or you're presenting to the CFO and you know 10% of what they do about the company financials.But a great PM has three superpowers in their back pocket:1️⃣ They can connect the dots across all those different teams as to how it all impacts the user experience and business strategy2️⃣ They can speak the language of each of those teams3️⃣ They’re humble enough to not pretend they’re the expertSo what’s the best way to communicate with other teams, particularly when you don’t all speak the same language? Two approaches:⭐ Use generic metaphors. Todd was the master at this, like comparing technical infrastructure to home construction. Something everyone will understand.⭐ My slight take on this: get to know your colleagues. Talk to them in language that gets them excited. Do they golf? Compare the weighting methodology in your algorithm to a handicap. Are they into design and interior decorating? Obviously you’d lay down the carpet before installing the furniture….just like sometimes it make sense to sneak in a small feature before working on a big one that’s dependent on it.What’s your go-to metaphor for explaining complex topics? I'm always looking for new ones!

I’ve spent 15+ years writing Product Requirements Documents (PRDs). Give me 5 minutes, and I’ll show you how to write a great one:—SIX STAGES OF PRD LIFECYCLEBefore we dive in, remember this: the PRD is not one and done.If your PRD is static, your team will build the wrong thing.A great PRD evolves over six stages to keep teams aligned and focused. Here’s how:—STAGE ONE - Aperture → Align leadership & team on directionThis is the starting point where leadership and product teams decide what’s worth exploring.→ It’s often just a speck of an idea — a pain point, market shift, or competitive insight.→ The goal is not to finalize anything but to agree on where to dig deeper.—STAGE TWO - Discovery → Identify the right problem to solveNow, the team goes deep into problem exploration.→ User research, data analysis, competitive benchmarking, everything to validate that the problem is real.→ Often, this stage results in a one-pager that outlines why this problem matters.—STAGE THREE - Define → Shape and scope the problemThis is the final convergence on the problem statement.→ The team nails down key constraints, trade-offs, and non-goals.→ You answer questions like: What’s in scope? What’s out? —STAGE FOUR - Design → Explore potential solutionsFinally, we move from problem space to solution space.→ Brainstorming, prototyping, and early technical feasibility checks happen here.→ You don’t pick just one solution yet, you explore different options.—STAGE FIVE - Deliver → Finalize and commit to a single solutionNow, the team is ready to lock in the approach.→ Engineers define technical specs.→ Designers finalize user flows and edge cases.→ PMs ensure cross-functional alignment.—STAGE SIX - Live → Launch, track results, and iterateThe product ships… but the PRD isn’t done.→ Measure adoption, impact, and user feedback.→ Analyze what worked, what didn’t, and what needs iteration.—TWO - PRD Process: Problem Space vs. Solution SpaceA great PRD is built in two phases:ONE - Problem Space → Define the ‘What’Team Kickoff → What problem are we exploring?Planning Review → Leadership alignment on the problem’s scope.XFN Kickoff → Gather cross-functional input before locking in the problem statement.TWO - Solution Space → Define the ‘How’Once the problem is crystal clear, this phase ensures you solve it the right way.Solution Review → Align on user experience, edge cases & tradeoffs.Launch Readiness → Lock in implementation details, success metrics & rollout plan.Impact Review → Iterate based on insights.—In a nutshell…A great PRD doesn’t just tell what to build, it also inspires teams to build the right thing.—And if you want to learn how to write the PRDs with examples, template, and step-by-step, go here: https://lnkd.in/e-nzkAwX

Holy moly. ChatGPT's Operator will be HUGE for automating SEO processes. Here's their AI agent analyzing competitors to create a content outline: Last week, OpenAI announced that they were adding Operator. This is their AI agent that can autonomously perform tasks. Operator can do things such as performing multi-step tasks. This includes interacting with web-based features such as performing searches and extracting data from them. This opens up a lot of new possibilities for SEO. For instance, instead of having to perform SEO research manually, you could specify exactly what you're looking to analyze and have an AI agent do it on your behalf. In this example, I had operator perform a competitive analysis for the query "how to ski moguls". After a pretty simple prompt, Operator did the following: 1. Performed a Google search for the query2. Correctly clicked to just the Organic text results3. Scrolled through and copied the text of every page4. Analyzed the extracted text5. Gave me insights on the most common sections competitors used and which competitors used them6. Created a content outline based on that dataSo without having to even perform a search - OpenAI's Operator was able to perform a complete analysis of a target query and provide me with an outline that I could start with. However, you could use it for much more. You could have Operator do things like give feedback on your own content, automatically analyze search intent, extract the top entities and much more. All through the use of an AI-agent. Now that are a few drawbacks to Operator in it's current form: 1. It requires the $200/month OpenAI subscription. Many people won't have access to that. 2. It's slow. Some tasks took it 10+ minutes to complete. 3. The output wasn't as detailed as I would have liked. However, it was a way to get real SERP data instead of just what's in ChatGPT's knowledge base. However, this is it's earliest form and it will only get better from here. From those that are looking to automate the manual work done in SEO, it might very well be worth the costs if it saves you a lot of time.

Then, use Search to validate: - Are there **industry trends** or **consumer sentiment studies** that align with its *Why*? - Find **3 real-world examples** of companies/initiatives that succeeded by operationalizing a similar *How*. - Identify **2 risks** where the *What* might misalign with the *Why*.2. But this is becoming impossible. We’ve worked with thousands of product teams and see the same trend over and over…1. We collect more qualitative data about our customers than ever. Customer stories get stripped of crucial details.🤔 The Trust Tax 🤔Every team thinks their slice of the data is the “right” one. Product decisions become political negotiations vs strategic planning.🏗️ The Technical Debt Tax 🏗️Dead-weight features persist without clear evidence of value or impact. Every deal I've closed over $208k started with a cold email. So, here's everything I do to make my emails land:First, get your deliverability up→ Mailforge for domains: https://lnkd.in/dkYh35fS→ Smartlead for warmups Second, after analyzing 200+ cold emails, here's a do's and dont list for email:1. EMAIL LENGTH: The average email length was 108 words. You want this to be 50-80.2. FILLER WORDS: Too many filler words. Things like "Reaching out just because" and "incredibly effective".3. THE USE OF I: The use of "I". Too many people talk about themselves.4. SUBJECT LINE LENGTH: The average from all emails was 5 words, we want this to be 2-3.5. CALL TO ACTION LENGTH: Here's a real example of a CTA: "To talk about this in more detail, how about we set up a quick conference call?"Here is how to fix this (in order):1. EMAIL LENGTH: Most emails I read had 3+ sentences explaining what their company does. You should be able to relay your company's impact in one sentence. Also, take out filler words!2. FILLER WORDS: Every email I read had too many filler words. These include:- "just reaching out"- "significantly better"- "an incredible ROI"- "I noticed that" (take out I and that.. get right to the point)There is no need for adjectives before the impact. To fix this, read your email before sending it and take out most adjectives as well as words like "like, just, because, only," etc..3. THE USE OF I: Many emails were talking about themselves by using different versions of "I". The "cheat" way to fix this is:- "I'm reaching out" >> "Reaching out"- "I noticed that..." >> "Noticed your.."4. SUBJECT LINE LENGTH: Most emails included 5-7 words in their subject line AND the name of the person they're sending it to. Unfortunately, this looks like spam. Here are examples I'd use for subject lines:- cloud costs- pipeline- your research paper- yesterday's news5. CALL TO ACTION LENGTH: Call to action length should not be a long run on sentence. Here are a few examples to steal:- "Worth exploring?- "Worth a chat?"- "Sound interesting?"- "Would it be a waste of time to chat?"- "Any interest?"These are a few ways you can start writing more impactful emails today.ps - here are the best email tools you can use today: https://lnkd.in/d3ExMcQY

A framework to understand the three most common types of product managers (via Rohini Pandhi):1. Pioneers: These PMs excel in zero-to-one, and thrive in environments where they need to build products from the ground up, with little to no existing infrastructure. They are good at using existing resources creatively to establish a foundation for growth.2. Town settlers: These PMs operate best in the growth stage of a product, when there is some level of infrastructure, and the focus is on scaling a product.3. City planners: These PMs are great at mature products. They are skilled at driving efficiencies at scale. Everything they do affects a large number of customers. This PM is very different from a zero-to-one PM.If you've met a PM and you thought they sucked, you may have the wrong flavor of PM.Here's our full conversation: https://lnkd.in/gMHBcgeG

I talked to 20 product leaders, and here’s what they told me: The age of the generalist PM is dead.If you're still trying to be an all-purpose PM in 2025, you're fighting a losing battle. Today's winners are specialists who dominate high-impact niches.Here are the 5 fastest-growing specializations they’re betting on (and how you can get in the game) :1. 𝐀𝐈 𝐏𝐫𝐨𝐝𝐮𝐜𝐭 𝐌𝐚𝐧𝐚𝐠𝐞𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭Successful PMs here understand how models work, manage data pipelines, and navigate ethical concerns. They recommend starting with hands-on ML projects and frameworks like TensorFlow.2. 𝐏𝐥𝐚𝐭𝐟𝐨𝐫𝐦 𝐏𝐫𝐨𝐝𝐮𝐜𝐭 𝐌𝐚𝐧𝐚𝐠𝐞𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭Ecosystems are king. PMs who can drive API adoption and manage developer communities are in high demand.Leaders pointed to companies like Stripe and Slack as models to study.3. 𝐅𝐢𝐧𝐭𝐞𝐜𝐡 𝐏𝐫𝐨𝐝𝐮𝐜𝐭 𝐌𝐚𝐧𝐚𝐠𝐞𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭"Regulation isn’t the enemy, it’s your competitive moat," one fintech PM told me. Focus on understanding compliance, building secure systems, and simplifying user experiences for non-technical customers.4. 𝐂𝐥𝐢𝐦𝐚𝐭𝐞-𝐓𝐞𝐜𝐡 𝐏𝐫𝐨𝐝𝐮𝐜𝐭 𝐌𝐚𝐧𝐚𝐠𝐞𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭"Solving global problems is where the smartest people are headed," another leader said. Learn about carbon markets and track key players like Climeworks.5. 𝐇𝐞𝐚𝐥𝐭𝐡-𝐓𝐞𝐜𝐡 𝐏𝐫𝐨𝐝𝐮𝐜𝐭 𝐌𝐚𝐧𝐚𝐠𝐞𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭Empathy is critical here. Top PMs embed themselves in healthcare workflows and prioritize patient outcomes. Shadowing healthcare teams was a tip that came up repeatedly.The key? Choose your specialization, then master the tools and frameworks it demands.Master one, and you won’t just ride the wave, you’ll lead it.#productmanager #productleader #productspecialisation #careergrowth

If I could go back and teach myself just one thing to become a better PM, it would be this: Manage risk using feedback.After 15 years, I’ve cracked the code and here's your guide to the Risk-Feedback Matrix: —Before we dive into everything, let’s first understand the 4 types of risk that come with every product decision:Value → Are we solving the right problem?Usability → Can users actually use it?Feasibility → Can we build it?Business Viability → Will this make sense for the business? Let’s talk about the matrix now.—𝟭. 𝗖𝘂𝘀𝘁𝗼𝗺𝗲𝗿 𝗜𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗿𝘃𝗶𝗲𝘄𝘀They’re your best tool for tackling:→ Value Risk: Understand what users truly need.→ Usability Risk: Spot real pain points and frustrations.At Affirm, one hour with a merchant revealed more about needs than weeks of analytics. But let’s be real: interviews can’t solve everything.They’re limited for Feasibility Risk → users can’t tell you what’s technically possible.And for Business Viability → pricing or scalability needs further validation.—𝟮. 𝗨𝘀𝗮𝗴𝗲 𝗗𝗮𝘁𝗮It’s the silent witness that tells you how users behave; not just what they say.→ Usability Risk: At Apollo, drop-off data pinpointed exactly where users struggled in our workflow.→ Value Risk: Validates whether your feature is solving the right problem. But data has its blind spots:It struggles with Business Viability Risk → usage patterns don’t tell you if your pricing works.And Feasibility Risk → it might highlight scale issues, but rarely uncovers the root cause.Pair data with qualitative insights for a full picture. —𝟯. 𝗦𝘂𝗽𝗽𝗼𝗿𝘁 𝗧𝗶𝗰𝗸𝗲𝘁𝘀 Support tickets are where the rubber meets the road.They’re gold for:→ Value Risk: Surface unmet needs.→ Usability Risk: Reveal friction points beta tests often miss.Fortnite’s tickets uncovered issues we never spotted in testing. But don’t expect too much from support tickets:They’re limited for Business Viability Risk → volume alone doesn’t explain profitability.For Feasibility Risk → they can highlight bugs but rarely explain technical constraints.So don’t just stop there…—𝟰. 𝗦𝗮𝗹𝗲𝘀 𝗙𝗲𝗲𝗱𝗯𝗮𝗰𝗸 Sales teams are your direct line to the market pulse.→ They crush Value Risk: What’s driving demand and what's not?→ They shine for Business Viability: Pricing dynamics, enterprise needs, etc.At Apollo, this led us to reposition our enterprise offering. Sales feedback has its limits.It’s weak for Usability Risk → sales focuses on the big picture, not workflows.And it won’t solve Feasibility Risk → details about implementation rarely come up.—Summarising everything...No single feedback channel solves everything.The Risk-Feedback Matrix balances them all:Customer Interviews → Strategic depth.Usage Data → Behavioral truth.Support Tickets → Unfiltered reality.Sales Feedback → Market pulse.

They don't teach you this in Product courses/boot camps:The most important part of your job happens in the 5 minutes after the meeting ends.When the engineer stays back to tell you what they really think.When the designer shares their actual concerns.When the sales rep reveals the real reason deals are stuck.The best product decisions are not made in roadmap meetings.They are made in hallway conversations, coffee runs, and Slack DMs.Your calendar might show where you were.But the spaces in between show where you learned.What's your most valuable conversation in those “in-between” moments?#ProductManagement #ProductLeadership #ProductStrategy

The Difference Between a Good Product Manager and a Legendary Product ManagerLet’s talk about impact.A good Product Manager ships features on time, manages stakeholders, and keeps the roadmap tidy. But a legendary Product Manager? They redefine the game. Here’s how:1) Good PMs meet customer needs.Legendary PMs anticipate them. They don’t wait for feedback—they study behavior, dig into data, and uncover problems customers didn’t even know they had. Think Steve Jobs making us believe we needed a touchscreen phone when keyboards ruled the world.2) Good PMs optimize for KPIs.Legendary PMs challenge the KPIs. They ask: “Are we measuring the right things? What’s missing?” They align metrics with outcomes that matter, driving sustainable value for both users and the business.3) Good PMs collaborate with teams.Legendary PMs inspire teams. They articulate a vision so clear, so compelling, that everyone from designers to engineers to marketing rallies behind it. They make people feel like they’re part of something bigger than just a product.4) Good PMs deliver solutions.Legendary PMs craft experiences. They zoom out to the ecosystem level. They think about emotions, journeys, and memories. Every touchpoint of the product feels intentional, from the first click to the last impression.5) Good PMs are decisive.Legendary PMs are bold. They don’t just iterate—they innovate. They take calculated risks that can propel their product (and their career) to new heights. They don’t fear failure; they learn from it.💡 A Thought for You:What’s one thing you’re doing today that pushes you from "good" to "legendary"?Remember, the best Product Managers are not just building products. They’re building legacies.Tag a PM who inspires you, or share your thoughts on what separates the great from the unforgettable. Based on our analysis of every word/phrase in ~7,000 pitch decks over the last decade, here are a few quick fixes for a seed deck:- Decks mentioning customer LTVs were twice as likely to raise.- References to unit economics were a good predictor of raise success.- Decks referencing “ROI” were LESS likely to raise.- Mentioning “milestones” in your deck was consistently a negative predictor.- Calculating and stating cost/time savings associated with your product almost doubles your chances of raising.- Referencing explicitly the size of the market and recent industry shifts in your deck significantly improves raise probability.- No correlation between length of deck and chance of raising.- Mentioning tax benefits like SEIS did not improve the chances of raising.

Here's my next little data gem for those of you following my earlier posts about Scott Galloway's comment that the big players don't advertise, on the Uncensored CMO pod with Jon Evans.A little primer - Professor Galloway said that “What do the companies that have added over $100 billion in value over the last decade have in common? They don’t advertise.” and "I think advertising outs you as not getting it.”In fact, most of the big companies you know and love as having added over $100B in value in the last decade advertise. A lot. Amazon is the biggest advertiser in the world. Netflix, Google, Facebook, WeChat and TikTok are all in the top 50 advertising spenders in the world. If we forget about Galloway's exact words for a minute (what does factual accuracy matter lol), the point I think he was making more broadly is that the best companies focus more on R&D and product excellence than they do on advertising and brand building.So I thought I'd run the numbers on that.Turns out, he's right - sort of. The companies that spend under 5% of their revenue on R&D do indeed spend much more on advertising as a % of their revenue. So yeah, advertising appears to be a cost that companies bear when they don't focus as hard on product.But dig a little deeper and there's nuance.The big R&D spenders are all tech and pharma. The medium R&D spenders are mostly auto. The low R&D spenders are mostly CPG.That's not an accident. Tech and pharma have monster margins. Especially software, because they can scale product at a very low marginal cost, and they can deliver the product for free on the internet and avoid retailers eating their profits. So there's a massive R&D payback when they innovate.The big question is whether, if P&G and AB Inbev spent 5x as much on R&D creating mindboggling new toothpastes and low-carb beers, would it have the same effect?Would they be able to reduce adspend 75% and still have competitive businesses?Nah. There are only very slight incremental gains to be made in toothpaste and beer. But we all need them. And those big CPG players are still massive, growing businesses and mostly good dividend stocks. As boring as we might think they are, they're not 'dead' or 'dying', they play an important role in the world, and if they cut their adspend and shifted it into R&D they would almost certainly see a massive decline in performance and stock value.What I take out of this is that the relationship between R&D and adspend is very category-dependent. If you're in tech, you can go hard on R&D and advertise less. It's not that the CPG companies aren't any good, its that they operate within the completely different dynamics of mature categories.Do I think the traditional companies should strive for better products? Absolutely. But Unilever won't be Meta anytime soon. And that's okay.

This is how to prepare for your next Product Management interviews, even if you have a weekend or a month to prepare 👇  Here's how I prepare for an interview:  🧐 Company Research: 5% (~1 day)Research the company’s products, culture, and social media. You’ll use this research on your stories for your interviews. 📝 Create Your Story: 10% (1~2 days)Write answers to these questions: → Tell me about yourself (include why you are a good fit for the role)→ Why do you want to join this company? (Use your company research)→ Why Product Management (for aspiring PMs)  ⭐️ Prepare Behavioral Questions: 20% (2~4 days)Use your stories to highlight transferrable skills to the job you are interviewing for. Tip: Prepare one story for every 2 Amazon leadership principles STAR+L framework: Situation → What/where/when happened? Task → What was the problem/task? Action → What did YOU do to solve it? (Spend most time here - be thorough and detailed)Results → What was the outcome? Lesson → What did you learn from this? (related to transferrable skills)  💻 Prepare for Product Design/Sense/Case questions: 40% (5~7 days)Use these 3 types of problems to practice: → Improve your Favorite product → Improve an app (something not popular) → Improve a non-tech product 🚀 Add a moonshot to your ideas Follow My STEP framework: → Ask questions → Share a GOAL → Users (3) - pick 1 → Painpoints (3) → Solutions (3) → Prioritize solutions → Metrics for success  ♟️ Strategy Preparation: 15% (1~3 days)Practice: Company → "What should [Company] do next?" Acquisitions → "Why did Microsoft acquire LinkedIn?" New Ventures → “Should Spotify make video games?” Framework - Use a mix of two things:→ 3Cs: Customers, Company, Competitors → Porter's 5 forces  📈Execution & Estimation questions: 10% (1~2 days)Execution questions are about the day-to-day of Product Managers, usually involving metrics → Metric changes: "X metric is down 15%, why?" → Measuring success: "How would you measure the success of X new feature?" Estimation questions are about market sizing with a top-down or bottom-up approach: → "How much revenue does YouTube generate per day?" The frameworks to use: Changes → Exhaustive list of all possible scenarios: External factors, internal, etc. Success → North Star (the Goal of the feature), Signposts (proof that you moved the NorthStar), Guardrails (don't harm the business - e.g., track revenue, subscriptions, etc.) Estimation → Start broad (e.g. # users in a country) and narrow down to specifics (e.g., Avg. time watched / person) ---- 💎 → Choose practicing > learning one more anything → Practice by writing all your answers and recording yourself TL;DR → 5% Research → 10% Yout Story → 20% Behavioral → 40% Product Design → 15% Strategy → 10% Execution & Estimation 👋 Need more help with your PM interviews? The North Star Metric is an extremely simple, powerful concept. But it's largely misunderstood.In this post:1. What is a North Star Metric?2. Common Misconceptions3. Recommended ClassificationLet's tackle them one by one:1. What is a North Star Metric?The term has been popularized by Sean Ellis, also known for coining "growth hacking" while being a growth marketer at Dropbox. In the Growth Hacking world, North Star Metric:- Is a single metric. The goal is to create focus.- Is easy to understand, enabling everyone to speak the same language.- Is customer-centric. Reflects how customers get value from the product.- Ideally, it ensures the value is sustainable, typically by forming habits.- Represents your progress toward vision/mission, fostering alignment and inspiring teams to move in the same direction.- Is quantitative. It relies on numbers, not opinions.- Is actionable. You take action based on how it changes. Otherwise, what's the point of tracking it?- Serves as a leading indicator of your long-term business success.2. Common Misconceptions It's important to clarify what the North Star Metric is not:- A few metrics. While a company may select a few key metrics, referring to them as "North Star Metrics" is a misunderstanding. Have you ever seen multiple North Stars in the sky?- A metric focused on business value, like LTV/CAC. This overlooks the essential aspect of being customer-centric. Besides, can your team truly get excited about solely growing revenue? - An Objective Key Result. OKR is a goal-setting technique. You can use Key Result to express an expected change in the Nort Star Metric. But it is important not to confuse these terms.- A strategy. A strategy is a cohesive set of choices, such as a Value Proposition, that reinforce one another and enable you to succeed in your chosen playing field. Your North Star Metric must align with your strategy.3. Recommended ClassificationI've come across various attempts to classify North Star Metrics. The best one I've found was proposed by Amplitude: https://lnkd.in/dWAa3aN6 Depending on the game you are playing, you might want to focus on:- Attention: How much time do your customers want to spend using your product? The time and recurrent use of a product can indicate the value people derive from it.- Transaction: How many transactions do your customers make in your product? Your goal is to help customers find the right product quickly and easily.- Productivity: How efficiently and effectively can someone do their work? Your goal is to help customers with specific tasks.Identifying the game you are playing is the first step toward finding your North Star Metric.Hope that helps. What are your thoughts?-P.S. You can download a free The North Star Framework 101 (12 pages, PDF) by subscribing to my newsletter: https://lnkd.in/dGK9wAKs

Is #productmanagement complicated, or do we make it crazily complex? Everyone interprets it differently, and it becomes a mess. Product Discovery → Uncover what creates value. Product Delivery → Discover what creates valueNow, clarifying the core of each fundamental:1. Product Strategy. Product Vision → Set the mission and where to land. Value Curve → Answer how to differentiate from the competition. Lean Canvas → Overview of key elements behind the core opportunity and business. Roadmap → The glue between delivery and strategy2. Product Discovery. Business Outcomes → Know the desired results to pursue. Customer Journey → Understand how your customer uses your product. Uncover Opportunities → Identify what could drive desired business outcomes. Prioritize and Focus → Pick one opportunity at a time. Ideate on Solutions → Get together with the whole team and come up with solutions. Test Assumptions → Uncover what you don’t know . Decide Based on Evidence → Invest in the winners and cut the losers3. Product Delivery. Product backlog → Organizing opportunities to pursue. Refinement → Sharpen backlog items to build a shared understanding. Alignment → Decide what to focus on and progress. Getting it done → Delivering Value. Using tech debt → Accelerate learning through tech debt. Review → Evaluate results and work with stakeholders. Improve → Take action to grow as a team--Let's simplify what others complicate.Subscribe to Untrapping Product Teams to rock the world!https://lnkd.in/gGXdQGnE#productmanagement #productmanager

I’ve used SEO to grow my startup to 9 million visitors in 3 years.But SEO marketing took me 10 years to learn. Here are 10 tips for writing the best SEO content in 5 minutes:1. Focus on Keyword ResearchChoosing the right keyword will make or break your SEO efforts.Use these tools to find hidden gems:• KWfinder• Answer The Public• KeywordsheeterGood content is nothing without the right keywords.Spend 80% of your time here.2. Just Get On BaseMost people fail at SEO because they go too big, too soon.Instead, choose keywords with:• Low difficulty scores• Low search volume• Thin competitionDon’t swing for the fences too soon.Just focus on hitting the ball.3. Long Keywords > Short KeywordsShort keywords look sexy but have too broad intent.Long keywords:• Target your ideal customers.• Convert higher.• Rank easier.Don’t try to rank for “Best Pizza”Instead, rank for “Best deep dish Chicago style pizza in NYC”4. Keep Your Enemies CloseStudy the articles that are ranking for your target keywords.Your competition will tell you:• How many products to list• How many words to write• What topics to coverNow do what they do, but better.5. Don’t Write Without an OutlineThe Superbowl is won in the preseason.Make an outline with:• Keywords to include• Article subheadings• Article inspirationGood SEO writing comes from good planning.Get organized first.Write second.Edit third.6. Make it Effortless to Publish OftenLike most things in life, SEO is a game of consistency.In the first 30 days:• Build an article backlog• Document your processes• Create a publishing scheduleConsistency doesn’t come from willpower.It comes from strategy.7. Study Search IntentYou have a goal in mind when you open Google.The most common goals are to:• Buy• Learn• DiscoverIn SEO, these goals are called “search intent.”Learn your audience’s search intent.Then help them achieve their goal.8. The BEST TED talk since Sir Ken Robinson's was published yesterday and here's WHY you should watch it. 🔴 In June 2006 Sir Ken gave what is still the most popular TED talk of all-time. It has 400M+ views across different platforms and despite being over 17 years old, it is still watched over 17,000 times every day. He spoke about educational reform but he didn’t drown everyone in data. He told stories (each one averaged just 2:06) but he didn’t show any slides. He had a serious point to make but he made the audience laugh every 29 seconds. It was a masterpiece in the art of public speaking and presenting. At TED Global in Vancouver earlier this year Irish economist David McWilliams took a page out of Sir Ken’s playbook, by using a very similar formula. I wouldn't be surprised if it goes on to be one of the most watched TED talks of all-time. David’s mission is to "demystify economics and make the topic accessible to audiences worldwide". In the same way that Sir Ken spoke about creativity and dance as he sought to influence his audience with his point of view on a broken education system, David used poetry to build his case for “THE POWER OF UNCONVENTIONAL THINKING" in economics. Here's a few minutes of his talk and some insights I picked out that might inspoire you for your next presentation: ⓷ His 18:06 minute talk followed a traditional 6-minute 3 act structure.1️⃣ Act I: Excite: “The Pressure isn’t the problem” (2:30) 2️⃣ Act II: Disturb: “The Value of Unconventional Thinking” (13:16) 3️⃣ Act III: Assure: “Poets v Economists” (2:20) 🤣 He made the audience laugh on average once a minute. 🗣️ He spoke at 142 words per minute which is the ideal pace for a presentation to an executive audience. 👏🏻 He had a 41 second “Cold Open” to capture the audience’s attention and connect with them via his nervousness about being the very last TED speaker of the conference. 🧠 Praised all the previous speakers first and summed up the conference theme in 14 words “the pace of change and what that's doing to us demographically, politically, socially, economically”. 🤷🏻‍♂️ He then explained how he was going to use an unconventional approach to making his case. ⚡️ He used the word “BUT” 15X to create tension and contrasts. 📖 He recited 2 beautiful passages of poetry flawlessly without any notes or autocue. ⏰ He told 4 stories. Each one averaged 2:02. 🎼 Gunning Fog readability score of 8.254 (the lowest I’ve ever seen for a TED talk.) 📈 NO SLIDES! 📊 NO STATISTICS. 💬 6 QUOTES. 💙 3 POEMS. 🎤 He closed by summing up his entire talk as a 27-word call to action: “If you want to understand the world a little bit more clearly, listen less to my tribe, the economists, and listen more to Yeats's tribe, the poets”. So HAPPY FRIDAY Linkedin folks!!! LINK to the full talk below – It's 18-minutes well spent.

I studied Palestine in college and grad school, spent time there, and discussed the situation with countless Palestinians. Continuing my previous posts (now tagged with #FactsOnTheGround), here is some information you may not know.Palestinians have always engaged in negotiations with Israel.The most significant concession Palestinians have ever made to Israel was the 1993 Oslo Accords.First, background: in 1948, Israel had ethnically cleansed and occupied most of Palestine. The West Bank and the tiny strip of Gaza remained in Palestinian hands.In 1967, Israel had captured the West Bank and Gaza as well, and placed them under military occupation. Since 1967, Israeli settlers began stealing and living on Palestinian land in those territories.Palestinians fought back against the military occupation of their land, through popular resistance and guerrilla operations like the Palestinian Liberation Organization (PLO) under Yasser Arafat. In the 1980s, the First Intifada (uprising) brought broad swathes of Palestinians together in a diverse protest movement. Israel violently crushed Palestinian protests, with Defense Minister Yitzhak Rabin famously instructing Israeli soldiers: "break their bones". (Pictures in comments.)Finally, in 1993, Israel agreed to begin negotiations by recognizing the PLO as a negotiation partner. In the 1993 Oslo Accords, the Palestinians made massive concessions: they would allow Israel to remain on the lands occupied in 1948, they would give up the demand for 1948 refugees to return to their lands, and they would allow the West Bank to be chopped up into a map that looked like swiss cheese, in which Palestinians only controlled small enclaves. These enclaves, the non-contiguous "Area A", have been compared to South African bantustans. (Map in comments.) The PLO even committed to disarming themselves and never fighting Israel again. By the way, the Israeli negotiator was none other than Yitzhak Rabin!And yet the Palestinians made all these concessions to just get one promise from Israel: that colonization and land theft would end. Israel could keep all the land they had already stolen, if they would just stop stealing land.Ever since the 1993 Oslo Accords, the PLO (now the Palestinian Authority, which governs Palestinians in the West Bank) has not fired a shot at Israeli forces. They even coordinate with Israel on "security" and deliver Palestinian prisoners to Israel.But Israel has not kept its promise. They have continued stealing land ever since the Oslo Accords. In addition to building more settlements, they have built a massive wall, which they say protects their border, but which actually cuts into the West Bank to grab more Palestinian land.Even Hamas offered to negotiate a state within the 1967 borders in 2018, but Israel mocked them. https://lnkd.in/gSJCy2zNWho refuses to engage in good-faith negotiations?

Battling the maze of product strategy? No more confusion! I've crafted a complete guide clarifying the 4 key elements of a sound product strategy. Let's solve the strategy puzzle together! 🚀This guide isn't like any of my blog posts that you read in 5 minutes and jump to the next one. I decided to share something that goes in-depth—20 minutes of reading time with the elements that would have saved me from many failures.Here are the 4 pieces that have solved the product strategy puzzle:1. Product Vision → Set the mission and where to land2. Value Curve → Answer how to differentiate from the competition3. Lean Canvas → Overview of key elements behind the core opportunity and business4. Roadmap → The glue between delivery and strategyLet me give you some more details.1. Product VisionA Product vision is customer-centric, memorable, audacious, and achievable. Once you have a meaningful product vision, you can challenge roadmap items whenever they are unrelated to the vision. You can also question feature requests that don’t help you get closer to your vision.2. Value CurveThe result of creating a Value Curve isn’t another artifact to ignore, but alignment on your key differentiation aspects. Don’t get stuck with details. Focus on deciding about the following:This technique is from the Blue Ocean strategy. It has four aspects in comparison to your competition:. Eliminate → Define what your product doesn’t offer. Reduce → Select a few product attributes to give less emphasis . Raise → Define the features you want to increase the value. Create → Start doing something unavailable in the market3. Lean CanvasThe result of crafting a Lean Canvas is a clear alignment of the most relevant aspects behind your business idea.Contrary to what you may think, it’s not about filling the blocks with information and moving on. It’s about making decisions on the business fundamentals, iterating, learning, and adapting.The Lean Canvas is extremely simple. But getting all the blocks together can be time-consuming. You need to make decisions on your audience, the problem you choose to solve, your unique selling proposition, etc. Such decisions require many considerations. Yet, a well-crafted Lean Canvas will help you accelerate decision-making.4. RoadmapConnecting the future with the present is hard. The product vision may inspire you to act, but you don’t know where to start. Then, the Lean Canvas brings some clarity to you. Still, you lack a tangible way of starting. That’s when the roadmap becomes the glue between the future and now. Yet, that can easily become a blessing or a curse.Prescriptive roadmaps will trap teams. The key lies in directions and not instructions. You can achieve that in multiple ways.My favorite roadmap template is an adaption of the format Now-next-latter. I used it for a long time and got value from it.--Read the full article to understand product strategy in-depth.

Here's why it's crucial to be well-prepared:1️⃣ Boosts Confidence: Research and practice instill confidence, enabling you to respond confidently and discuss your qualifications.2️⃣ Company Alignment: Prior research about the company ensures your answers align with their values, goals, and culture.3️⃣ Tailored Answers: Preparation allows you to anticipate common questions and craft compelling, relevant responses based on your experiences.4️⃣ Highlight Relevant Achievements: Reflect on your accomplishments and select the most relevant ones for the role you're applying for.5️⃣ Overcome Nervousness: Thorough preparation helps alleviate anxiety. Familiarity with potential questions and practicing responses leads to better performance during the interview.To enhance interview readiness, follow these tips:📌 Research the company: Learn about their mission, values, recent achievements, and industry trends.📌 Understand the role: Align your experiences and achievements with the key skills and qualifications mentioned in the job description.📌 Practice common questions: Use the STAR method to structure impactful responses.📌 Conduct mock interviews: Seek feedback from a friend or mentor.📌 Dress professionally: Choose attire that aligns with the company's culture.📌 Plan logistics: Be well-prepared with the interview location and necessary documents.To prepare effectively, allocate dedicated time each day or week leading up to the interview. Your routine may include:✅ Researching the company and role✅ Practicing interview questions✅ Refining your responsesAdditionally, enhance your industry knowledge and rehearse your elevator pitch.Remember, thorough preparation is key to success in any endeavor, including interviews. If you want shortcuts to product discovery, stop here and read no further.I decided to put together a comprehensive product discovery guide. It will take around 35 minutes of reading time to digest the content.I wrote this guide to share stories and practices to give you applicable insights on product discovery.Here are the key elements you’ll come across:1. Business Outcomes: Know the desired results to pursue2. Customer Journey: Understand how your customer uses your product3. Uncover Opportunities: Identify what could drive desired business outcomes4. Prioritize and Focus: Pick one opportunity at a time5. Ideate on Solutions: Get together with the whole team and come up with solutions6. Test Assumptions: Uncover what you don’t know 7. Decide Based on Evidence: Invest in the winners and cut the losersNow, a quick deep dive1. Business OutcomesAgree on the desired result you want to achieve. But don’t leave it fluffy. Define the trade-offs the organization is willing to make as well as the aspects you cannot sacrifice.Example: Increase growth rate even if that increases customer acquisition cost, but do not compromise customer satisfaction2. Customer JourneyThe critical aspect of a customer journey is to ensure your team members build a shared understanding of your customer journey. That’s the job you need to get done.3. Uncover OpportunitiesTo uncover hidden opportunities, you need to learn from real end-users. The closer you are to them, the better. In B2C, it tends to be easier than B2B, but what you’re about to read can apply to both models.Techniques: Interviews, data analysis, shadowing, etc.4. Prioritize and FocusTrying to explore as many opportunities as possible simultaneously will stretch teams too thin and create little to no value.The real challenge is picking one opportunity per team and exploring them. I know you believe you’re the master of multitasking; we all believe that. But the reality is different.Focus is what will enable you to create value sooner. 5. Ideate on SolutionsThe next step isn’t crafting a high-fidelity solution and getting approval from management. That’s not going to help you become empowered. As you agree upon the opportunity, it’s time to decide on the solution you want to explore.Solution ideation is the moment where the future meets the present. You’ve identified problems worth solving and want to craft solutions worth building.6. Test AssumptionsStrive to identify assumptions you lack evidence of and are business critical. As you uncover them, run experiments to test the assumptions and decide whether to pursue the initiative or not7. Decide Based on EvidenceMost of your ideas won’t work as expected. The magic is separating good ideas from bad ones. I recommend running small experiments and scaling them fast.Read the full guide for more insightsLet’s rock the product world together

$100,000,000.That's about how much ARR our first demo "scripts" at Gong closed.I partnered with our VP Sales to write these sales demos for the first few years.Here are 9 sales demo tips I learned through it all.1. Show the most powerful feature FIRST.Most SaaS sellers don't do this. They 'build up' in a 'ramp up' demo.Wrong.Flip it upside down and show the top feature upfront.Let your buyer's head spin (in a good way).Unpack from there.2. Solve exactly.Want an easy way to speed customer decision making?Solve their pain on a 1:1 basis.No more, no less.Don't complicate it by showing all you've got."We can also..." leads to overwhelmed buyers.3. Catalyze a decision.What's the point of a demo?To inform? Educate?Neither. It's to catalyze a decision.What's the 'next' decision your buyer needs to make?Agree on that before the demo.Then, demo in the context of helping them make that decision:"My goal is to get you so FIRED up about what we do that at the end, you're willing to sponsor a meeting with your CMO, or you tell me I missed the mark. Fair?"Catalyze. Don't inform.4. Frame the problem.Summarize the buyer's challenge and impact before the demo.Then validate it:"Based on what you told me, you're going to LOVE this."Let your buyer know they're in the right place.5. Orient them to the screen.Most buyers 3 minutes into SaaS demos:"WTF am I looking at?"Before you click around, explain what they're seeing.The confused mind says 'no.'6. Spend 10s framing the pain a feature solves before showing it.Remember how I said to frame the problem before the demo?Do a mini version of that for each feature you show.What's the pain it solves?Spend 10s summarizing that.THEN the feature will land with ease:"Earlier you said xyz is a headache. Here's how we solve that."7. Tell a customer story after every key feature.Want your buyer to think about your demo for days?Tell a customer story after each click path."Here's how X customer is using that workflow to solve Y pain"People remember stories and forget click paths.8. Ask a question after every key feature.Stop and have a conversation.Here are a few questions to test:- How does that compare to how you solve this today?- To what degree is this resonating so far?- To what extent do you see that solving x pain?- What benefits do you see showing up in your world from that?9. End your demo with "What excited you most about what you saw today?"Transition to discussing next steps.Hope these help you.P.S.Take your SaaS demos to the next level with the (free) $100M demo script: https://lnkd.in/gmc4K4kZ

I’ve been thinking about something over the past few days, time to share and get some reactions. There was the Internet, then search, then mobile and social, and now we are at the start of another inflection point with AI. Language is at the heart of these inflection points, never more so than today. How people and companies use language to communicate, transact, work or play will be ever more critical and dynamic. Phrase is central to how the biggest brands in the world navigate language today, and in the next five years we see our role evolving to be more strategic than ever. We will provide the technological backbone that powers the localization ecosystem for companies, well beyond the boundaries of where localization sits today. Already our customers tell us they want global employees to be able to localize information in real-time. In the future, companies will need to connect systems, easily configure them, analyze and automate quality, reliability, tone and content, all to enable a degree of collaboration way beyond what’s possible today. Imagine a world where entertainment and commerce adapt language, tone and content in real-time to the user. Today, despite how far we’ve come, language is relatively static: every visitor to a website, every viewer of a movie, every reader of content, every service agent in the field, every employee in your business, is consuming broadly the same content, localized once (hopefully!). Content, even localized, is not dynamic. Tomorrow, content could be truly dynamic - every visitor, viewer, reader, agent and colleague might see or read contextualized, hyper-personalized content wherever and whenever they interact. Why should two potential customers see the same product description, if they have different buying criteria? Every website in the world could be rendered in real-time, based on the user; the same for entertainment, software, and all content types. Perhaps true hyper-personalization would have been too expensive to be viable in the past, but the advent of AI will make this possible. This isn’t about a 10X or 100X order of magnitude change to how language is used in business: this is real-time rendering of the entire internet and all content, so every interaction with your content is in a different language presentation in real-time. This is a 10^9X explosion of language… The implications of this for businesses and creators of all types are profound. And Phrase will help you navigate it. Because enterprises require many systems of record, content originates in many places and needs to be disassembled, checked, quality-controlled, regulated, re-assembled and monitored. Phrase will be the enterprise backbone that helps companies maximize the opportunities in the coming explosion of language.

Are you doing Product Management or Bullshit Management?Companies may hire you as a Product Manager and quickly limit your abilities to get your job done. Gathering requirements from stakeholders to solve their wants instead of delivering on end-users' needs2. Keep an extensive Product Backlog to keep all requests from stakeholders registered instead of removing items unrelated to your current goals3. Preparing frequent performance reports for management instead of evaluating the impact of your deliverables4. Striving for consensus to please all stakeholders instead of seeking the best option for the product5. Signing off all items delivered by the team to ensure it matches your quality control instead of empowering them to reach goals6. 12 Product Management hacks that feel illegal to use. Number sixth will surprise you, though you probably saw it working already:• If multiple teams/products are presenting at a Product review meeting, please try to go last. Stakeholders will be tired and less likely to ask difficult questions.• If a remote meeting is going nowhere and you have no input to offer, do a British exit. You probably have better things to do.• If someone offers to help, take it! You probably have too much work as it is.• If you need to cancel a meeting, inform your peers in the chat, but keep the meeting on the calendar - you will have some time for actual work.• Book time in your calendar for lunch and focus work - spending 8h on meetings in a day is probably not productive and I'm pretty sure is a crime against humanity.• Sometimes dire issues will resolve themselves if ignored for some time. However, it's a craft to tell things that only appear dire from those that are actually ticking time bombs. Proceed with caution!• (Probably) Every Product Manager ever has no idea what she/he is doing. Don't be afraid to make it all up as you go along, project confidence on the way, and if it works out, let stakeholders believe it went exactly how you planned it from the start.• (Almost) all metric impact estimations, that you provide with your proposed product updates, are GUESStimations. Everyone knows that. Don't be shy to give your best (erm... educated?) guess!• Being silent for a long time in a room full of different opinions will allow you to speak last. You will have the benefit of knowing the full picture and appearing as a leader who speaks the wisest.• If you don't know how to answer a question, kick the can down the road: "Let me check my data after the meeting and get back to you". Most likely the person asking the question will forget all about it once the meeting ends.• While AI and ChatGPT are not a solution producing reliable answers to any query, give them a go. Maybe AI will give you just the numbers/research you need. Fact-check it though, so far at least the Bing bot is super unreliable for PM stuff for me. • Have a healthy emotional detachment from your team and product. Being stressed and carrying too much will drain you, making you a worse Product Manager.Follow me for more traditional and unique advice on how to be a Great Product Manager. My course and blog will also help with that! Check the links in the comments section of this post.#productmanagers #productexperience #productmanagement

ChatGPT can help you turn 10x more interviews into job offers. But 99% don’t use it correctly.Here’s how to use ChatGPT to stand out and land the offer: 1. Ask it to generate your practice questionsPractice is the most important thing to do in your prep. So use the right questions to be as effective as possible. Ask ChatGPT:"What are the top 10 questions I am likely to hear in an interview for [Role] at [Company]?"2. Do your researchThe best candidates espouse the company values and have lots of knowledge about the company. ChatGPT can cut that time in half. Ask it:"What are the company values at [Company] that I should espouse in my interview?"3. Inspire your interview responsesChatGPT can help inspire your first draft response - personalized to you. Direct ChatGPT to your LinkedIn, then ask the question. Like this:"Read my Linkedin [URL]. Then craft a compelling response for the question: [Question]."4. Have it grade your responsesThen you can write your own response that's better than it's draft for you, and have it give feedback. Ask ChatGPT:"If you were [Interviewer Role], what would you think of my response below to the question [Question]? [Response]" 5. Practice your technical interviewsFor technical interviews, it can literally grade your code. So you just ask it to help you prepare. Say this:"Help me prepare for a technical interview on [Topic] by being my coach. Ask me a practical question, and await my response."6. Have it be your case partnerCases are often the hardest thing to practice. ChatGPT can be your partner. Ask it:"Let's simulate a [Case type] case interview. Please be the interviewer and ask me a question, and we'll go through several steps together"7. Research your interviewerKnowing your audience always helps. You can avoid saying things they disagree with. Just ask ChatGPT:"What has [Interviewer] said about [Function]? Or what can you infer about their points of view from their online presence?"8. Brainstorm great questionsAll interviewers end with Q&A. Make the best use of the time. Ask it:"What are the most impressive questions I could ask [Interviewer Role] at the end of an interview for [Role] position?"9. Understand skeptical areasOne of the most powerful things to do is to proactively address your weak areas. But they're hard to know yourself. Just ask ChatGPT:"Read my LinkedIn (Link) and the job description (Link), what are the weakest elements of my candidacy?"10. Write thank you notes68% of hiring managers value thank you notes. So, after the interview - go back to ChatGPT. Ask it:"Can you help me craft a compelling thank you to [Interviewer]. Mention [Thing you talked about]."Power tips:1. Use Bing: It's free, and they just opened it up to everyone yesterday. It can search the web in real-time to answer all these questions.2. Use 1 Chat: This will allow ChatGPT to build context and use all the conversation above to make its responses better.
