Design Systems vs. Product Strategy 💥Why do some companies crush it with their design system while others barely give it a thought? The game-changer? The desire for an incredible product. ➡️🚀 PRODUCT-LED COMPANIESYour business is your product.* Have a design system.* Prioritize user experience, speed, innovation* Easy-to-use product means less work on support* Prefer metrics + data analysis over subjectivity* Gather feedback and iterate fast* Flexible, agile org structure* Cross-functional collaboration* Optimized design processesProduct-led companies understand that having a design system means HIGHER speed, development velocity, and ROI. 🛠️ PRODUCT-ENABLED COMPANIESThe product enables you to sell your service.* See the design (system) as a cost, not an investment 🤑* UI kit is "enough."* Top-down hierarchy* A lot of decisions makers + a slow process* Prioritize stability over speed and flexibility* UX is important, but the design is not so much🟡 PRODUCT-SUPPORTED COMPANIESThe business can function without a product. However, factors like industry, competition, and customer expectations can make these supplementary products increasingly vital for a business's success and customer satisfaction.* Design system, what is that? 😅* Siloed work environment* Top-down hierarchy* The pace is slower* Lack of innovationNo matter which industry you're in, adapting fast is crucial as technological advancements happen week by week. #designstrategy #designsystem #business #designmanagement

"Well WHY didn't they say anything?". Managers often jump to assuming lack of skill/motivation. That *could* be the issue, but more things might be at play:They don’t participate in the process• Don’t know they should participate• Lack of sense of ownership, inclusion, etc.• Have low confidence in the process• They don’t find it valuable / helpful• Don’t understand the goal of the process• Have developed workarounds that “work fine”• Believe someone is participating on their behalf• Unclear what is involved, time commitment, etc.• Overwhelmed with other work and other demandsThey want to “stop the line”, but don’t• Power/decision disparity• Feel like they will be overruled• Worried they’ll get pulled into a bunch of meetings that go nowhere• Are concerned they don’t really have enough information to make a call• Don’t feel they have an objective way to make the call• Compare the situation to other risks, and figure “this is normal”• Figure someone is taking care of it (but they aren’t)• Unclear of how an escalation might work, and who would be involved• Don’t want to be seen as obstructionist or “not a team player”• Feel that “it will not make a difference because the problems are elsewhere”• “Too late in the process, it will create too much trouble”• Inadequate training on how to identify and report problems• Perceive a lack of support from management or colleaguesThey do raise issues, but it doesn’t lead to change• Assume it was taken care of• They address the issue, but their fix never reaches customers• Team appears to find a resolution, but doesn’t act on that resolution• Other people didn’t understand the issue they raise• The resolution/fix gets forgotten• Lack of follow-up or accountability measures• Communication breakdown between teams or departments• Prioritization conflicts with other projects or tasksThey are fine with how things are (but perhaps shouldn’t be)• Lack the domain expertise (e.g., the product area)• Lack the functional experience (e.g., learning experience design)• Inadequate training on how to identify and report problems• Have trouble “seeing the big picture” and connecting actions to outcomes• Figure someone must be taking care of the issue• Disconnected from the negative externalities of their decisions• Based on the status quo, assume this is normal

So it turns out that sustainability in advertising isn't just good for the planet, it's good for your brand too.This week I hosted one of our Sky Media Breakfast Clubs on 'The Impact of Sustainability in Advertising'. It was World Earth day last week and we've recently launched our Sky Zero Footprint fund competition for the 3rd year running - a £2m media fund to get more sustainability brands in the spotlight. We're on a mission to use the power of TV to tackle climate change and we're really excited to see the impact that the brands we work with can make. But at the same time, ClimateX released some research this week saying that sustainable messaging in advertising had declined. Eeek. With the cost of living crisis inevitably taking up much of people's attention, it appears that the future of the planet is taking a back seat in the advertising world. Perhaps for some brands it was a nice to have. But what if brands knew that done right, campaigns that are driving forward the sustainability agenda could work even harder than anything else?With 2 years under our belts, we have learned quite a lot about what works in this space, and what doesn't. But to check we weren't just drinking our own cool-aid, we thought we would do some research to objectively see whether sustainability in advertising does actually work, or not. We also thought it would be useful to see what the dos and don'ts are to help anyone planning to enter the Footprint fund this year. This is my favourite chart from the research. I always have a favourite chart! Out of 54 brands tested, we took the most engaging and persuasive with sustainability messaging and the most engaging and persuasive without. It's pretty clear that the ones 'with' perform better. Amazing news for the future of advertising and even better news for the future of the planet.If you'd like to know more about the research you can revisit the recording as well as some exclusive content here 👉 https://lnkd.in/e6sAgN2qAnd if you're keen to enter the Sky Zero Footprint Fund all the details you need are here 👉 https://lnkd.in/giahAR3g#sustainability #advertising #media #research #tvadvertising Sky Media UK

Thanks to my brave friend, John Quinn (Wake Forest), who did this in Moscow in 2017, and is cured, I knew about this procedure. It has a success rate of 73% to 80%. I could not wait for the FDA to approve something that has been practiced in the US for 30 years, just not for MS patients. This is NOT an experimental treatment. Product Design is not UX DesignEver since I dove into the world of design in 2004, it's been an exhilarating ride watching design roles evolve and adapt! I've closely followed these changes, and now I want to share my thoughts about the differences between product design and UX design roles.Product Designers play a central role in digital product creation teams, helping to shape the future of technology.🏣 Many tech companies that develop products have established "product trios" consisting of the product manager, tech lead, and a product designer. These teams collaboratively drive product discovery, strategy, and delivery.🗯️ In many job postings, the terms Product Designer and UX/UI Designer are used interchangeably. However, there is a key difference between these two job descriptions: Product Competence. 💭 Product thinking and product sense are the essential skills that set Product Designers apart from UX/UI Designers.If you want to put it simply: ✨ A product designer is a UX/UI designer with strong product competencies.While many former UX/UI designers have grown into the product design role, the product designer is not another term for a UX/UI designer. It's a different role with different requirements. The scope of the role is more extensive than before. It is about impacting the product vision and roadmap and driving it together with the other functions. Product designers with product competencies will have a more significant impact on the product.Here are some examples of relevant product competencies for product designers:Product Vision – Ability to imagine and design the future and drive proactively towards itProduct Strategy – Ability to define, influence, and build support for long-term product strategyProduct Thinking – Skill of knowing what makes a product useful — and loved — by peopleProduct & UX Metrics – Ability to understand and define metrics to track and measure customer and business benefits.Product Delivery – Ability to break down the product vision into meaningful increments while delivering high-quality productsProduct Discovery – Ability to understand and use tactics and frameworks to inform product decisions about what to buildI want to wrap this topic up with an inspiring quote from Julie Zhuo, from her great article about product thinking:"Nurturing and developing product thinking is therefore relevant to anyone in the business of covering trends or building great products. And in a time where technology companies focus more on product-led innovation than, say, financial engineering, it’s not just an academic or technologist exercise — it’s one that actively contributes to market value, too." […] As with all skills, it can be nurtured and developed; it’s not just an instinct one does or doesn’t have (and even instincts are trained, after all)"You can find the article from Julie Zhuo and some other good reads on this topic in the comments. #productdesign

Product management is like cooking. You need the right ingredients. 9 ingredients you cannot afford to leave out.Cooking well is a combination of love, ingredients, and recipe. Even if you have the secret recipe from a Michelin-star Chef, you'll get a horrible outcome with the wrong ingredients.Here are the ingredients you cannot miss:1. Product Vision2. Product Strategy3. Product Goals4. Business Model5. Value Proposition6. Identify Assumptions7. Test Assumptions8. Learn from Failures9. Measure OutcomesLet me give you more details and some shortcuts ;D1. Product VisionMy favorite vision is from J.F.K. "Before this decade is out, we land a man on the Moon and return him safely to the Earth"It's audacious, inspiring, achievable, and measurable.Template: bit.ly/3KTdV7I2. Product StrategyDefines the constraints to play the game. You'll find many strategy templates.My favorite is the Lean Canvas by Ash Maurya because it gives all you need in a single canvas A great strategy enables focus on what matters and empowers decision-making.Template: bit.ly/3L82OsB3. Product GoalsGive bite-size missions for teams. A reasonable timeframe for a Product Goal is between one and three months.Example: bit.ly/40Ja5ni4. Business ModelUnderstanding your business model enables you to connect the dots.Although Lean Canvas gives you critical answers, it doesn't cover the business dynamics.I see value in using the BMC by Strategyzer5. Value PropositionHow does your product solve your audience's pains? Answering this question is vital.Without market fit, you have no business.I use the Value Proposition Canvas by Strategyzer6. Identify AssumptionsDo you know what you don't know? Identifying desirability, feasibility, and viability assumptions is mandatory. Yet, a common trap lies ahead. Not everything deserves your attention. Prioritize the most critical ones and test only them.Use the Assumption Matrix by David BlandTemplate: bit.ly/43UeM0D7. Test AssumptionsNaming assumptions isn't enough. The secret lies behind understanding the proper method to create the learning to justify investing or dropping the idea.Read the Testing Business Ideas by David Bland8. Learn from FailuresYou won't get everything right. Failures are just part of your journey.Shift from failures to experiments and strive to learn and drop bad ideas quickly.Experiments: bit.ly/3LbgkvPDocumenting results: bit.ly/41npJ959. Measure OutcomesUnderstanding that features are a means to an end is critical. Measuring the result is what enables you to create value.Read this bit.ly/3V9kGqD by Paweł HurynHow do you cook your product management dish?Follow me for more insights#productmanagement #leadership

Most PMs aren't good user interviewers 👎Many have a tendency to ask surface level questions:Do you like our product?Why don’t you use our products? What features do you want in our product?These questions can lead to surface level answers and can sometimes annoy the customers when you fail to really connect with them at a deeper level.Getting user research right is a crucial step in making sure you are finding the right problem to solve and that your product will truly meet the needs of your target users and create customer and business value. 🔑 The key to doing powerful user research is to turn your customers into storytellers of their experiences!Here are 10 powerful user research questions to uncover the nonobvious insights of your users ✨:1️⃣ Paint a storyCan you tell me the last time you encountered [PROBLEM], and what you did to resolve it? 2️⃣ Get the juicy details Ask for more details to build up the full context of the story. Why did you do what you did? 3️⃣ Probe for the pain What was the worst part of having to deal with [PROBLEM]? How acute was the pain?4️⃣ Gauge Willingness-to-PayWould you be willing to pay [MONEY, TIME, EFFORT] to solve this problem? Why or why not? How about I raise or lower this by %?5️⃣ Evaluate current experience To what extent on a scale of 1-10, would you say the current [PRODUCT] is helping you solve [PROBLEM]? Can you elaborate on your score?6️⃣ Check for alternativesHave you considered other alternatives? Why or why not and how was your experience with them?7️⃣ Check for the ceilingWhat would you say it’s an 11-star experience in solving [PROBLEM]? What makes it so wonderful? 8️⃣ Check for the floor Have you ever had a bad experience when solving [PROBLEM] or using [PRODUCT]? What happened and what makes it bad? 9️⃣ Gather value propsHow would you describe [PRODUCT] to your friend or colleague?🔟 Test hypothesis If I gave you a [PRODUCT + MOCK], what parts of the solution would you like? Why or why not? To what extent it’s helping you with [PROBLEM]?As you are hearing customers’ stories and their responses to your questions, make sure to repeat back what you heard.Reframe in your own language to make sure you accurately understand what they are saying and make them feel heard. 🍒 For more resources, see this Question Bank from Maze: https://lnkd.in/gXpxyi5M***💬 What user research questions do you find most effective? Share them with us and we may feature them in future posts! #ProductManagement #Careers #UserResearch

Last year, we shared our point of view that businesses are facing a crisis of disconnection - disconnected systems, disconnected people and disconnected customers. Of these challenges - the most challenging one for growth - is the disconnect between companies and their customers. The channels we have used for years to attract customers and build relationships are becoming less effective. So if you’re a marketer or salesperson and feel like you’re working harder but seeing less traction, you’re not alone. Just look at the data.Organic reach from social media continues to decline; ten years ago, organic reach for the average Facebook page was 16% and today it’s about 8.5%. While Instagram’s rates are better, it’s also dropping year after year.Search isn’t driving traffic like it used to; in 2020, 65% of Google searches ended without a click. Zero-click search and advertising have changed search usage over the past few years, and we should expect Gen AI and ChatGPT to accelerate this trend. Email is getting noisy; 33% of marketers we surveyed said low open rates was one of the biggest problems they’re facing. Plus, we found that email response rates dropped by roughly 40% last year. The bottom line: Channels are oversaturated with content and ads, and it’s pushing buyers away. As a result, companies are seeing their organic traffic decline, their lead gen slow down, and their conversion rates drop. That means it’s time to adapt. Businesses need to evolve their GTM strategy to match the way buyers want to connect today. This is what we’ve been digging into at HubSpot and we’ve found one guiding principle: Value > Volume. 83% of marketers believe it’s more effective to create higher quality content less frequently. Buyers are already flooded with content, so adding volume isn’t the answer. Instead, you should focus on adding value. Here are a few thoughts:Dig into insights: Chances are, 20% of your content is driving 80% of your connection with customers. Look at the data to understand which channels and formats are performing best, and double down there. Deliver deep relevance: Once you know which channels and format work best, you need an exceptionally relevant message to stand out. Use customer insights to see where they are in their journey, recent interactions, etc. to really tailor the experience. Leverage Gen AI: You could use Gen AI to write 100 blog posts per day, but that doesn’t mean you should. (Value, not volume, right?) "We're 100% aligned!" Oh really?"We finished our OKRs! We're aligned!" Oh really?"The team agreed on action items!" Oh really?Alignment is about cultivating a collective sense of purpose and direction, not achieving total consensus or conformity/consistency. It is a continuous process of discovering and rediscovering common ground, shared values, and shared understanding—pragmatically and in context. Shallow alignment is low-stakes, easy to achieve, and fits neatly on a slide. Deep alignment embraces the idea that multiple, sometimes conflicting, values and truths exist. It is messy and requires people to agree to disagree. Shallow alignment avoids tension. Deep alignment inherently involves tension and friction, hence why deep alignment requires psychological safety, time to diverge, and time to get real.Shallow alignment is expensive to maintain and crushes creativity. Deep alignment is strategic, resilient, empowering, and complex.Alignment is not a checkbox. Alignment is a system in dynamic equilibrium, constantly adjusting and responding to changes. There is overall balance, but minute by minute and day by day, things are shifting, adapting, bending, and reconfiguring. There's no such thing as "100% alignment". The minute you've left the meeting, the system is rebalancing based on new information.Too often, teams rely solely on alignment proxies—goals, lists, budgets, and priority lists. Teams stress "commitment" over understanding. While these artifacts may have emerged from a momentary (or perceived) state of alignment, they are not, by themselves, "alignment." The system shifts. Are these still the right goals? Have we received new information? Do we share the same conviction as we once did? We may agree to see our bets through, but that doesn't mean our alignment system is in equilibrium.The point? Participating in the continuous journey of alignment takes work, vulnerability, and safety. It is not a checkbox. And we should look beyond the shallow forms of alignment that predominate in a business context.

𝗔 “𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗱𝘂𝗰𝘁 𝗠𝗮𝗻𝗮𝗴𝗲𝗿” 𝗶𝘀 𝗾𝘂𝗶𝘁𝗲 𝗮 𝗰𝗼𝗻𝗳𝘂𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗽𝗼𝘀𝗶𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗲𝘀 𝗶𝗻 𝗺𝗮𝗻𝘆 𝘀𝗵𝗮𝗽𝗲𝘀 𝘀𝗶𝘇𝗲𝘀 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗿𝗲𝘀𝗽𝗼𝗻𝘀𝗶𝗯𝗶𝗹𝗶𝘁𝗶𝗲𝘀! I remember when I first got the title in a little start-up, I’m pretty much sure someone called me a “Product” and not a “Project” Manager by mistake and the title somehow stuck. As I found this interesting, I started exploring the position and decided to grow to become a true Product Manager! Two years, many learnings and confusing interviews later, I reached my goal and became a Product Owner in an international company. During that time and towards today, I saw the position being formalized and clarified, however at the same time different subdivisions emerged. Throughout the years I managed to identify the following aspects of Product Managers:𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗱𝘂𝗰𝘁 𝗠𝗮𝗻𝗮𝗴𝗲𝗿 - Classical generalist, pure focus on Product. Responsible for choosing the priorities of what to change in the Product. Sometimes also known as “Business Product Manager, which did really confuse me when I bumped into this phrase a few weeks back. This type of Product Manager has a few subvariants:▶️ 𝘗𝘳𝘰𝘥𝘶𝘤𝘵 𝘖𝘸𝘯𝘦𝘳 - Ideally, a PM in Agile Scrum. Practically, often a junior PM or some joke on the intended set of responsibilities. It all depends on what the employer pictures a Product Owner as. ▶️ 𝘎𝘳𝘰𝘸𝘵𝘩 𝘗𝘳𝘰𝘥𝘶𝘤𝘵 𝘔𝘢𝘯𝘢𝘨𝘦𝘳 - A generalist PM focused on growing the Product's user base. This can be the same or confused with:▶️▶️ 𝘗𝘳𝘰𝘥𝘶𝘤𝘵 𝘔𝘢𝘳𝘬𝘦𝘵𝘪𝘯𝘨 𝘔𝘢𝘯𝘢𝘨𝘦𝘳 - A growth PM that manages the growth by Marketing means (ads) rather than changes in the Product itself. This position might also be known as Performance Marketing Manager.▶️ 𝘗𝘳𝘰𝘨𝘳𝘢𝘮 𝘔𝘢𝘯𝘢𝘨𝘦𝘳 - A merge between a Product and Project Manager - so on top of the regular PM complexity, this person is also responsible for timely update delivery and progress reporting. Nothing to envy, this is literally two positions in one with not enough time to do either with high quality and full dedication.𝗧𝗲𝗰𝗵𝗻𝗶𝗰𝗮𝗹 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗱𝘂𝗰𝘁 𝗠𝗮𝗻𝗮𝗴𝗲𝗿 - Specialized PM, which holds Technical expert knowledge. Responsible for choosing the priorities of what to change in the Product and how to implement it. Unlike a classical Product Manager, this position may include being a formal leader of the team and being directly responsible for the solutions and progress. So a merge of Product Manager, Project Manager and Engineering Manager, but this time for good reasons. Being a tech expert helps to combine those roles and choose where the person should focus one’s energy. Here are the subvariants I am aware of:....well, they are outside this LinkedIn post due to the character count limit. Read the rest of the post, exclusively on Medium! Link in the comments section :)#productionmanagement #productexperience #productmanagers

🤹 So, you started a new Product Management position and you inherited someone else's backlog? 𝗛𝗲𝗿𝗲 𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝟭𝟬 𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗽𝘀 𝘁𝗼 𝗺𝗮𝗸𝗲 𝘀𝗼𝗺𝗲𝗼𝗻𝗲 𝗲𝗹𝘀𝗲'𝘀 𝗯𝗮𝗰𝗸𝗹𝗼𝗴, 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗼𝘄𝗻:1) 😱 Don't panic - It can look scary and overwhelming initially. Don't worry, it will get better in time it just needs time. Rome wasn't built in a day, nor was it refurbished in a similar time span. It will be nice and shiny in a little time!2) ❄️ Let it go for a sprint or two - While you adapt it to your vision, just start with using whatever's on top. You need time and the work on top of the backlog will usually be the most polished. Though a quick sanity check and asking some basic questions to the team and manager won't hurt!3) 💩 Start clearing poor quality from high-quality epics - Look for stuff that is months, if not years old. You should be able to quickly identify obvious trash, i.e. feature requests from people who already left the company.4) 🐞 Limit the number of bugs - I would say keep the 10 most important ones (ask the team about prioritizing those) and forget the rest. Brutal, yes, but let's face it, they were never going to be fixed anyway!5) 📏 Shorten the backlog to a manageable number - I believe that I read in the Scrum Guide that 50 is the maximum you should aim for. Anything beyond that is simply a lie. Keep the 50 with a FIFO mentality.6) 🗒️ Establish a draft epic format - Now that you have the numbers in order, let's start working on quality. Establish your ideal epic/PRD/task (whatever it's called in your backlog) format. Be sure to include impact hypothesis and tracking requirements among many others!7) 👥 Confront the format with the team - Ask them if they are comfortable with the format. Maybe it will be better to have tracking requests as a separate subtask rather than in the main body. You both work on those, it would be best if you all feel comfortable with it!8) 🦾 Adapt all the epics in the backlog to the established format - The new standard won't mean much if it isn't immediately applied, now does it? It will probably require a lot of work and research. It will also uncover not well-thought-through epics or plain bad ideas. This is simply the additional level of the cleaning process!9) 👨‍🔬 Make your own due diligence on the remaining epics — Perhaps your predecessor had already some impact hypothesis, metric estimations, etc. DON’T ASSUME (S)HE DID A FINE JOB AND YOU CAN JUST USE IT AS IS. It’s yours now and if you are going to make decisions based on them, you better make sure you can also take full responsibility.10) 💡 Start adding your own initiatives — Now that the backlog is clean and yours, it’s time to wow your stakeholders with initiatives, that have no legacy history behind them. Good luck!Hope you found those useful! Do you have any other advice to share? Sound off in the comments!#productmanagers #productmanagment #backlog

You are the head of marketing -- the CEO asks: "what's our website conversion rate?"Pause. Breathe. And NEVER provide a blended conversion rate for the entire website. Instead, here's how to answer:"Great question, CEO. We have many different traffic sources and they all want different things. Which traffic source would you like to learn more about?" CEO will likely come back with: "Okay, so what are our different traffic sources?"Break it down like this:1. Demand Capture - Paid Search / Affiliates2. Demand Capture - Pricing Page / Demo Request3. Education / Exploratory - Main Website Pages 4. Education / Exploratory - Blog & ResourcesFinally, your CEO will ask: "And how do you measure each?"Now THAT's the question you want to hear!Answer like this:"For demand capture sources, that traffic typically converts at around 10% on average, given the stronger buying intent. We tend to measure it like sales..."- Landing Page Conversion Rates- Conversions to Opportunity- Opportunity to Revenue"For education / exploratory traffic, we expect less than 1% conversion rate. We know conversions might happen, but it's not the primary thing they want, so we measure success by paying attention to leading indicators like..."- Assisted Conversions- Chat Engagements- Avg. Session Duration- New Visitors vs. Returning- Keyword Rankings- Brand vs. Non-Brand Clicks Friends don't let friends give the CEO a blended website conversion rate metric. Share this and save a marketer's job today. #marketing 🚀

This email got me: 70% open rate, 45% reply rate, and a 20% positive reply. Here’s why:Subject line:Short and intentionally boring. I’m a fan of using their name if not done in a cringy way (i.e. “quick question Alex”)1st line:It clearly states ONE problem. A lot of emails get this part wrong. Either the problem isn’t clear, it’s not relevant, or they mention too many problems.2nd line:Clearly states the impact of that problem with relevant metrics to back it up (this is how you use metrics the right way - with context!).3rd line:States the benefits, Like a TLDR. CTA:It’s clear, short, interest-based (i.e. not asking for time), and it’s at the end of the email! Too often CTAs are buried.Two takeaways from this email:#1 - This is PROOF that if you’re targeting is done right, personalization isn’t always necessary. I'm can send this to 100000s leaders. I don’t care because it’s relevant.#2 - it follows a simple framework: problem, the impact of that problem, their solution to that problem, and call to action.

As a Partner at Sequoia Capital over the last 34 years, Douglas Leone has cemented his place in venture hall of fame with:- Investments in Nubank, Meraki, ServiceNow and Wiz- Leading the Sequoia franchise for a glory decade- Making 78 partner investmentsLast year Doug joined 20VC for a masterclass on the biggest lessons from his 34 years at Sequoia staying at the top. I broke down the 9 main lessons from the show: 🔥👇1. Never Get Complacent: Yesterday’s Success Does Not Determine TomorrowsSuccess is the greatest danger: it has to do with yesterday. Tomorrow is the only thing that matters. It's always about the next partnering opportunity with founders.2. How to attract and retain the best people?Show everyone respect. I spoke to dozens of CFOs about how they make buying decisions.Companies small and large (included F200) are all saying the same 4 things: 1. Their biggest fear is buying shelfwareThey’ve all been burned by tech that’s purchased but never adopted. Change management and end user experience is key. Sellers need to give confidence in the implementation plan to make this as seamless as possible.2. Timing is more important that pricing They want to find ways to start small (free trial, paid pilot) to mitigate the risk of shelfware. Then they have their FP&A teams write up an ROI analysis for the larger deal later. Inside-out business cases to complement the seller-created plans.3. The business case has to be rock solid At smaller companies, CFOs have to sell your business case to the board. There are always competing priorities and decisions need to be justified. You can’t just deliver a solid business case and expect to win, you also have to work with their team to build their internal version, and do the buyer enablement to make it easy for them to resell.4. CFO references are critical They want to know why their peers are using it and why they love it. Be proactive in setting up these references, don’t wait to be asked. This shows control and confidence – CFOs like both.De-risking deals is the name of the game right now. Do everything you can to convey “why now” in every conversation. #revenue

Maslow's Design System Hierarchy ▲💥1️⃣ Functional needs (the base - it has to work!): First and foremost, the design's gotta do its job. Think user-friendly, accessible, and downright usable for everyone. Make it easy peasy.2️⃣ Reliability needs (consistency is key): Next, we want our design system to be reliable and consistent on all devices and platforms. Nobody likes a flaky friend. So, handle errors with grace and keep them maintainable.3️⃣ Flexibility needs (adapt fast): Our design should be a bendy yogi, ready to adapt to user preferences and different contexts. This means customization, localization, and stretching to fit all screen sizes and input methods.4️⃣ Efficiency needs (speedy Gonzales): We want users to zip through tasks with the help of our design system. That means cutting out the fluff, trimming workflows, and offering nifty features like shortcuts, search, predictive suggestions, onboarding guides, and templates. 5️⃣ Visual needs (make it unique): Layout, typography, colors – let's make it pop! Sure, it's not priority numero uno, but an impressive design (system) gets way more love and adoption than the one that's just there to exist! 6️⃣ Sync (collaborate): 🌟At the tip-top of the pyramid, let's empower users to grow! I'm a big believer in education, so I suggest tools, sharing knowledge, and giving our coworkers a high-five for every milestone. 🙌 What would you add/remove from this pyramid? 😊#designstrategy #designstrategy #designmanagement

How to cold call (5 steps):1. Opener: Permission based. Acknowledge the cold call (no tricks), ask for a moment to say why you've called and most importantly let them know it'll be quick."Hey Sally, This is Mike with Deel, I know you didn’t expect me to call you this afternoon, do you mind if I take one minute to tell you why I called you specifically, and then you can tell me whether or not it makes sense to speak?"2. Pitch: Purely focused on the problems your persona faces. Has nothing to do with you, your company or your product. The goal is to start a conversation."Typically when I talk to other [their role] that are [insert relevance] a couple of challenges are top of mind:1. Challenge 12. Challenge 2Are either of those relevant to you or did I miss the mark?"3. Objection handling: All your objection handling should start the same way. That way you're never caught off guard. My favorite framework is "actually, that's why I called." For example, how to handle not interested:Actually, that’s why I called – most folks aren’t actively looking for a solution but thought you may be curious how similar [persona] is achieving [positive business outcome].4: Closing: Cold calls should not last more than 5 minutes. Once there's interest go for the close. “Awesome [Name], well look I know I called you out of the blue. Would you be against taking a look tomorrow afternoon or [Day] in the morning?”5: Qualification: Counterintuitive but save qualification questions for after the meeting is booked. "Before you hang up would it be alright if I asked 2 questions so we make the most out of our time tomorrow?"Bonus: If you have a great conversation but it's not the best fit, ask for a referral.“Hey [Name], All good, I see it’s not a fit. But I see you used to work for [Previous Company]. Would it make sense to reach out to anyone there? Or is there anyone you could put me in touch with?”What would you change?Oat milk is the best milk,-Mike GPS This structure is a Frankenstein of strategies from Jason Bay Jed Mahrle Nick Cegelski and Avi Mesh give them a follow, their content is legit!#sales #coldcalls #salestips

An entire MBA fits on one page. But you better know the math cold.Like & comment if you want the excel.6 topics:*1) Unit economics*This builds from product-level unit economics to company financials.Quantity per unit, growth in units. Price per unit, growth in pricing. Cost per unit, marketing spend per unit. Get you return on ad spend (ROAS), customer acquisition cost (CAC), LTV/CAC, gross margins, unit contribution margins, and more. This model sets up two diverging products: A higher gross margin (GM), higher marking cost, low pricing power, low growth product.Against a lower GM, better pricing power, better growing, lower unit market cost product. To show how the dynamics of the two play out over time. *2) Accounting*Unit drivers flow into revenue, variable COGS, and variable SG&A.Then to EBITDA, EBIT, EBT, Net income & EPS. Simple cash flow statements & balance sheets reconcile D&A to inflation-adjusted replacement CapEx, debt levels, & GAAP PP&E. You have to be fluent in accounting to look at public companies. Not because accounting itself matters.But because accounting can obscure what matters. And your task it to translate GAAP into meaningful business logic.*3) Operating ratios*Unit drivers output financials, financials output overall business ratios. Revenue growth, EBITDA growth, EBITDA margins. Contribution margins (= change in profit over change in revenue).Net debt-to-EBITDA, net debt % of EV. *4) Valuation*You can drive valuation on multiples or a DCF. Which are equivalent:The discount rate minus the growth rate determines the 'terminal multiple.'Using a P/E instead of a DCF simply uses next year as the terminal value. This model drives value from the DCF, and maps that to the multiples to show how they connect.*5) Corporate finance & DCFs*DCF math attached using the CAPM.The summary is a beta from historical returns, add the cost of debt. To get the WACC - the theoretically correct discount rate. DCFs are extremely assumption laden - you can get out almost whatever you want. But realistically.Your banker (or analyst) will make the math work out to a 7-13% discount rate. Depending on the risks, industry, markets - and what they want to achieve.*6) Sensitivities & the hard part*The last section sensitizes the equity value and multiples to unit and price growth.Bringing unit drivers full circle to valuation outcomes. Ultimately, the hard part isn't the math. You have to be fluent in the math, its nuances and its limitations.If not, you will lose out to those who are.But once you are, you also have to shift focus. To the hard part.Which is always in filling in the numbers.If you're investing, that means thoughtful views on unit drivers; on how, when, and why they shift.And if you're building, it means making the numbers on the page happen.That's all for now.Like & comment if you want the excel.

For years, we’ve been told that the best sellers talk WAY less than their peers on calls with prospects.🤔 Welp, here’s some data that counters that.Taken from “The Jolt Effect,” the image below shows that sellers interrupt and talk-over prospects MORE in wins than in losses. And additional data they’ve got shows that rep talk time is 58% in wins vs. 52% in losses. 📣 Let’s dig into the interrupts & talking-over.They point out that the best sellers are actively listening and participating in what they call “cooperative overlapping.”This is when the listener starts talking along with the speaker, not to cut them off but rather to validate or show they’re engaged in what the other person is saying. Saying things like:“Absolutely.”“Yeah, I hear what you’re saying.”“Yup, makes sense.”“That’s interesting.”“Hmm… okay.”Interesting stuff! They go on to say that there’s a time and a place for strategic silence in calls. But on the whole, engaging in a true two-way conversation is a commonality of top performers. I cut experiment run time by weeks when I introduced CUPED at Booking..com but before you get too excited, let me tell you the truth about CUPED and other similar techniques.Lots of people talk about techniques like CUPED as if they can 10x the velocity of all sorts of experiments. Sounds too good to be true, right? It often is.CUPED is not a magic bullet that can speed up any experiment. It only works in a very specific context: when you have reliable pre-experiment data that can predict future behavior. For example, I used CUPED to test the performance of accommodation listings (e.g., hotels) that had been with Booking for a long time and had stable metrics. But when I tried it with new users, it was a waste of time.So how do you know if CUPED is worth investing in for your experiments? Here’s my golden rule: ask yourself if your users (experiment units) interact with you regularly and consistently. The key here is that you need pre-experiment data that's predictive of future behavior. If yes, then go ahead and test CUPED as a way to reduce noise and increase power. If no, then don’t bother and stick to the basics (or message me to get into some fun chats about more complex options ;) ).Without useful pre-experiment data, kiss the value of techniques like CUPED goodbye 👋 but if you've got it? Hop on that bandwagon! Share your experience with such techniques below!

The only way I know of to develop better product intuition for your own product is to:1. Constantly use your product as a real customer would2. Constantly research your target customer -- their habits, problems, environment, way of doing things, psychology. 12 ways to make the above practical in your week-to-week:1. Use the product daily as a real user would [15 minutes / day]2. Watch one or two user research or replay sessions [10 minutes / day]3. Check your key usage metrics dashboard [5 minutes / day]4. Interview a prospective client and ask them to describe a specific workflow related to your product [30 min / week]5. Email or slack 1-3 existing clients with a specific feedback question [30 min / week]6. Read notes from recent sales calls or user interviews [30 minutes / week]7. Read customer feedback queue tickets [30 minutes / week]8. Read the latest data analysis on user behavior on your product [30 minutes / week]9. Read industry blogs / articles related to your product area, ideally about either customer learnings or from the perspective of customers [30 minutes / week] 10. Explore competitor products as a real user world [2 hours / month]11. Try selling the product yourself, or tag along on a sales call [2 hours / month]12. Read 3 books a year relevant to the psychology of your customers [5 hours / week] If you did EVERYTHING on the list above, it would take you 10-15% of your working hours. Doing half of them would be more like 5% of your week.This is tiny investment for 1) honing your product intuition 2) making better prioritization decisions 3) gaining greater conviction in your work and frankly 4) having way more fun in your work.

Many people in companies are using design systems, but we keep focusing only on making things easier for designers and developers, as if they're the only ones who matter. But here's the catch: we also have too many sources for design and code. As a result, even those who want to understand the entire system have difficulty.We're talking about a whole lot of noise. 🤯 And when you've got that much noise, you're not optimizing anything. You're just making a mess.So, what's the solution? ⤵1️⃣Discuss the design system process with real movers in your org - your "local" leaders in each department. They know how the team works, what they need, what they miss, and their goals. 2️⃣Forget about an ideal setup. Every consumer has different needs.3️⃣Define the audience (consumers)Listen to their feedback, address their pain points, and do your best to optimize their workflow.4️⃣ Make a list of their main tasks. For example:DESIGNERS* Create and copy components* Design Principles* Know the latest status* Onboard new designers* Research* Rules for design* Maintain Figma Libraries* Documentation* Themes* Testing* HandoffDEVELOPERS* Know the latest status* Maintain repositories* Onboarding new devs* Copy/edit the code* Setting the guidelines* Pushing changesStakeholders* KPIs* Have an overview* Resources, costs* Buy in* Know the latest statusOther departments* Know the latest status* Tone and voice* Design Principles* Downloading assets* Have an overviewHonestly, you can't please everyone.Building a design system balances users' needs and your organization's goals.#designsystem #designamanagement #designstrategy #designleadership

Biggest mistake salespeople make selling to CFOs?Here's one of them:Selling ROI without regard to 'hurdle rate' or priority.Your proposal could be a SLAM DUNK ROI.Doesn't mean a CFO is going to buy.Great ROI cases have to fit two other criteria to work:1. They have to align with a top priority.If your ROI case is rock solid, but doesn't align with the business's top priorities..."Talk to us in six months" is all you'll hear.CFOs and their colleagues don't have time to act on everything. Even if it's guaranteed ROI.They stick to priorities.2. It has to be above the CFOs' 'hurdle rate'The hurlde rate is the minimum acceptable return needed to even get the CFO's attention.Your proposal guarantees a $1M ROI?Awesome!But if that CFO manages a $65B P&L...That's peanuts. Doesn't matter if it's easy or guaranteed. It's a distraction.Get all three right and you've got gold.P.S.Here's a free cheat sheet that includes 7 tips for selling to CFOs (based on an interview I did with three CFOs of billion-dollar companies).Grab it free here: https://buff.ly/3kNy5H7

Two years ago, I bought a creative agency for $300k.It’s now getting $10,000,000+ acquisition offers.Here are my top 8 lessons for entrepreneurship, leadership, and startups:BE GREEDY WHEN OTHERS ARE FEARFULI bought the business during peak COVID fears in the U.S.Revenue for the business had cratered.The founders wanted an experienced CEO plus extra months of cash cushion for the small team.I was willing to provide both.BUY BUSINESSES SOLVING LONG-TERM PROBLEMSThis agency specializes in video creative for paid social.Good creative is the lifeblood of growth for companies advertising on social media.We turned the business around in 9 months.Companies need help with short-form video.BUYING A SMALL BUSINESS CAN LOOK LIKE BUYING A JOBI was the hands-on CEO before transitioning to a great operator.Be ready to get your hands dirty or have a CEO who has already operated a larger business than the one you are buying who can step in immediately.CLOSE THE WHALESIn our first year, we built winning creative for billion-dollar brands like Instacart, Square & Grubhub.That helped us sell to leading eCommerce brands like Dr. Squatch soap & Thrive Causemetics.Each early big client logos made every subsequent sale easier.DON’T OUTSOURCE RECRUITINGCEOs need to do cold outreach to attract critical talent.In my case, one key hire led to 10 more. That started from a cold email.Early team sets culture so it pays to stay involved in recruiting.DEFINE YOUR IDENTITYOver time, we ran more winning creative experiments.Generating winning results became our identity.Our identity aspired to be the Navy SEALs of creative on paid social.We worked with the largest brands on social & held ourselves to elite standards.EXPERIMENT WITH PRICING MODELS EARLYAs we became very good at winning creative, we could price our services based on performance - a rarity for traditional ad agencies.Find a pricing model that aligns with the value you deliver to customers.FIND YOUR IDEAL CUSTOMERNot all business is good business.After testing different-sized customer engagements, we learned that our skill set best matched LARGE advertisers on social (over $300k/month in social ad spend).Experiment often and find your ideal customer.-If you are a large advertiser on paid social, check us out and join our waitlist below:https://lnkd.in/gi_hfae2

The 1st call between a pre-Seed startup & investor 📞Fundamentally, pre-seed comes down to 3 things - team, product & distribution - the journey from pre-launch to early PMF.So the basis of a 1st call, which on average is 30 minutes long should be focused on the following 👇1/ 4 min ‘Hello & Intro’ 👋2/ 10 min ‘Team’ 🧑‍🤝‍🧑3/ 7 min ‘Product’⚒️4/ 7 min ‘Distribution’ 📈5/ 2 minute on the ‘Raise’ 💰1/ INTRO - once you’ve touched on where you are based and what the weather is like today, you move into the more formal introduction. This should be ~2 mins long (each). You should have an ‘elevator pitch’ based on the three key components of your company. For example:“20VC operates at the intersection of venture and media. We have a $140m Fund, investing from pre-Seed to Series B. We invest globally, with a heavy lean towards Europe and the US. We are sector agnostic, investing in consumer social all the way through to enterprise SaaS. Why? Because we’re exceptional at 2 things - network and distribution. The podcast gets 35m+ downloads a month and we have interviewed ~3k+ guests. There is nobody in tech we can’t get too.”I then have more detail on the Why & How we are the best at what we do. But this comes later.2/ TEAM - the most important. It’s a cliche, but for a good reason. At pre-Seed, Team is tangible, everything else (pretty much) is theory. So what makes you the right team to go after this category. What unique insight do you have that nobody else has seen? What's your 'unfair advantage'? Talk about your childhood - built a computer game when you were 12? Sold bottles of Prime at school for £10 when you bought them at the Emirates for £5? This part will take up the majority of the call because it is the most important!3/ PRODUCT - expand on the unique insight you have and what you’ve started to build. Talk about V.1 of your product, the insertion point, your ICP. Why are you entering the market THIS way? Going from product to platform? Why will your customer use it quickly / frequently?4/ DISTRIBUTION - personally, this is the part I really love. How do you get ACCESS to your first 10 customers / 1k users? Showcase an advantage only you have? A key contributor to a sub-reddit group you can leverage to on-board your first 100 design-partners / ambassadors? A great Product Hunt launch lined up? Inherent flywheel dynamics built into the product? For example: “20VC has a great guest on its show. Great guest brings new listeners. New listeners learn about the 20VC Fund. Listener is building a business or has a friend building a business. They reach out to 20VC. 20VC invests.” So you can see our north star metric is having the best possible guest on the show.5/ THE ASK - I should already know how much you're raising from the deck i've seen or the person who's introduced us. So talk about how the round is going, what your dream investor should offer and why this is the right amount to raise to get you to early PMF.

Advanced principles for clear thinking:Everything we create, everything we do, it all starts with our thinking. Clear thinking drastically improves odds of success in all departments of career & life. While clear thinking is quite rare, it can be developed with practice.1) Essence first. Not story. Not analogy.Most people get seduced by great analogies & exciting stories.Clear thinkers don’t *form* their thinking via analogies. They identify the essence of the issue, in their specific context. Then, they use analogies as one of their inputs.2) WAYRTTD.“What Are You _Really_ Trying To Do” is a simple but powerful tool to make you pause & identify your real goalMost people move too quickly to How & When to do a given task. But the task isn’t the goalClear thinkers have built a habit of asking themselves WAYRTTD.3) Message vs. Messenger.Most people first evaluate the messenger & they don’t evaluate the message enough. The apparent superior/inferior status of the messenger clouds their thinking.Clear thinkers practice their best to evaluate the message separately from the messenger.4) Tough to communicate.When dealing with close choices, most people pick the choice that is less stressful to communicate & reject the choice that will seemingly require a tough conversation.Clear thinkers resist this bias & communicate the correct choice as kindly as possible.5) Probabilistic thinking.Most people think in terms of “X will happen” “Y will not happen”. They are forever locked in the jail of binary thinking.Clear thinkers see the future as probabilistic. They think in terms of approximate probability that X will/won't happen & decide/act accordingly.6) Metacognition.Most people don’t understand much about why they think & feel what they think & feel. They are mere passengers on their own train of thoughtClear thinkers develop a habit of observing their own thinking, analyzing it with curiosity & adapting it accordingly7) Opportunity cost.Most people decide what to work on solely by ROI.ROI = (Value created – Cost of your time) ÷ Cost of your timeFor decisions that matter greatly, clear thinkers consider Opportunity Cost.Opportunity Cost = Value of optimal option – Value of chosen option8) Ownership mindset.Most people’s thinking gets clouded by all sorts of noise & drama in their environment.Clear thinkers approach whatever they choose to work on with an ownership mindset. So they are more focused on the greater goal and they adopt a collaborative approach.9) Control over ego.Most people attach their identity to their ideas & thinking. This makes it hard for them to adapt their thinking & see reality as it is.Clear thinkers are not immune to the ego, but they are able to control it enough so they can iterate towards correctness.10) Not perfect.Finally, clear thinkers are aware that they are not perfect. It is often the recognition & reminder of this flaw that gives them greater clarity than others.~

I’ve spent 1000s of hours studying the top 0.2% of storytellers.People like Tolkien, Rowling and Jobs.Here’s what I learned:***Start with the end in mindWhat do you want the outcome of your story to be?• Reader falling in love with a character• Customer buying a product• Friend laughing hilariously• Investor giving you moneyIf you start with the end in mind, the intro and middle naturally funnel to that target.***Shape your storyHumans gravitate to structure. Luckily there are tons to wrap around your story:• Hero’s Journey• StoryBrand• Three ActThe goal of a story structure is to let the characters shine through.The attached image is the actual plot outline JK Rowling created for the 5th Harry Potter.***Lead with actionMost people amble on in backstory for 10 minutes.This is a waste of time.Find the interesting parts of your story, jump right to it, and provide as little backstory as possible.***Make it emotionalPeople make decisions based on emotion.But it’s impossible to make your audience feel everything.Nail down 1-2 emotions and direct the entire story to amplify those.***Build a worldTolkien said: “The story maker proves a successful sub-creator. They make a secondary world which your mind can enter.”A few tips to create your world:— Use rituals— Define its laws— Create shared language (you don’t need to create a brand new language like Tolkien did!)Think about all the words you learned from the Harry Potter world: muggle, the spells, dementor, and many more.Storytelling is how you introduce people to the world you’ve built.***Sell the transformationGreat storytelling is about change.When Steve Jobs introduced the iPhone, he said:“Every once and a while, a revolutionary product comes around that changes the world.”He didn’t promise apps or amazing cameras — he promised change.***Slow downBefore your story’s climax, pause to force your audience to lean in.When speaking, stop talking for 3 seconds.When writing, make your paragraphs longer, add more sensory details, and layer on the drama.Force your audience to hang on to every word.***Build to one momentThe entire story should be designed to amplify one moment.But what is the moment about?Change — I once was this, but now I’m this.***Develop your own creative processJK Rowling uses outlines. Stephen King doesn’t.Neil Gaiman writes every day. George RR Martin doesn’t.Brandon Sanderson writes best at 11pm. William Faulkner at 9am.The lesson? Do you, but be consistent.***I hope you enjoyed that.If you want to become a better storyteller, try my free newsletter with 51,538 others.1 storytelling tip each Saturday morning: https://lnkd.in/dRppPN-y

The product death cycle is a terrible trap. Many PMs forget about the "Why" and become waiters.To please everyone, a typical waiter collects and waterfalls all "feature requirements" to the team. But no matter how much the team delivers, it gets only worse. Features shipped in a hurry do not solve anyone's problems and do not drive the expected business results.The feature factory.Fortunately, you can prevent or escape that trap. Try this:1. Respect your vision and strategy2. Do not let your customers design solutions3. Test your ideas with MVPs and prototypes4. Push back on things that are handed downNow in detail:1. Respect your vision and strategyAsk yourself:- Why are you building this product?- Where do you want to be in 2-5 years?- For whom are you building it?- What's the unique value of your product?- How is it aligned with the company's vision and strategy?- What are the key tradeoffs? - What are the customer jobs (functional, emotional, social)?- How will it create value for the business?- How can you get there?Recommended books:- Business Model Generation by Alexander Osterwalder- Plating to Win by Roger Martin-2. Do not let your customers design solutionsFocus on understanding their problems, needs, and desires in-depth. Interview them regularly, map opportunities, and brainstorm possible solutions. Make sure the designer and engineers are included.This will let you build a shared understanding and stay open to different perspectives. In particular, the best ideas often come from engineers.Recommended books:- Continuous Discovery Habits by Teresa Torres- The Mom Test by Rob Fitzpatrick- Escaping the Build Trap by Melissa Perri-3. Test your ideas with MVPs and prototypesLearning by delivering is expensive. Run small experiments in a front-of-funnel to validate ideas in the cheapest and the fastest possible way before selecting them for implementation.Key risks you need to tackle:- Value. Will our customers desire\love it?- Usability. Will they be able to use it?- Viability. Can different parts of our business support it?- Feasibility. Is it possible?- Ethic. Should we do it?Recommended books:- Inspired by Marty Cagan- The Lean Product Playbook by Dan Olsen- Testing Business Ideas by Alexander Osterwalder and David Bland-4. Push back on things that are handed downRespect your stakeholders, but don't let their authority influence what is important. Question solutions and push back on things that are handed down to you."A person's success in life can usually be measured by the number of uncomfortable conversations he or she is willing to have" - Tim FerrissRecommended books:- Influence Without Authority by Allan Cohen and David Bradford - The 7 Habits of Highly Effective People by Stephen M. R. Covey-What are your thoughts? Credits: the product death cycle was described by David Bland.

The worst way to open a follow-up prospecting email:Hi Bryan - I never heard back…Correct. They didn’t respond. Probably because the email wasn’t relevant. Or it didn't catch their attention.That's OK. "If at first you don't succeed, dust yourself off and send again."OR maybe they meant to respond but never got around to it, and now they feel guilted (or annoyed). Avoid that.Here are 3 replacements you can use instead:1. Cut the line entirely. Just get into the reason you're reaching out. 2. Bryan – Here's the thing: [insert reason for emailing / why relevant]."Here's the thing" is what's called a 'signpost' — a prompt that grabs attention because it makes the reader think "What thing?" Then they read on to complete that internal question.3. Bryan, imagine this: [describe relevant problem Bryan has, and briefly how you can solve it]."Imagine" immediately makes your Reader picture themselves in what ever follows. Ideally that's a problem you help them solve.There are a million more, but they key is opening the email in a way that is unusual (aka a pattern interrupt).Then getting to the point immediately.No need to remind prospects it's the 4th (or 14th!) email you've sent.——✅ Want more tips for turning your inbox into a pipe gen machine?Join Kyle Coleman and me for our live session "How to write un-ignorable sales emails that convert" next week.You'll get expert-level tactics and examples for grabbing attention and motivating buyers to act now.PLUS All attendees will get 5 email templates in your own mini-playbook you can put into play immediately. 95% of sales people are approaching selling on LinkedIn wrong. The whole idea of commenting on posts and getting people intrigued through building content and engaging only works for certain personas that are actually active on LinkedIn.But what about the rest of us?Here are 6 tactical steps for selling on LinkedIn that have helped me book 10 meetings in the last 3 weeks on this channel alone:Step 1: Build a targeted persona listGet very specific here with your ICP and try to narrow it down to SEVERAL factors based on your data (Example: B2B SaaS, 200-500 employees, must be using Salesforce, Sales team >10, growing 10%+ MoM)Step 2: Build a LinkedIn specific sequence. Important note: Never pitch first. Sequence consists of:1. Connection Request (Blank ones perform better)2. Asking an open ended question.3. Send them a benchmark report/guide that’s relevant with no ask.4. Continue viewing their profile/endorsing skillsStep 3 (this one is the key): Once/If they accept your request, ask an open-ended question that sparks curiosity. Wording is extremely important.This simple yet powerful question got me a 30% reply rate:“Hey [name] - mind if I ask your expert opinion on a potentially silly [Relevant topic] related question?”Step 4 (also very important): Ask the “silly” question, but make it a genuine question that will lead to a conversation around the topic your solution can help with. Make sure it’s not a sales-y question like:“Would you be interested in getting 15 more leads a month?” This will ruin it.Step 5: If they are still engaging with you and you nailed the question to bring up the topic you help with, that’s when it’s time to ask how they are currently managing that process.It’s all about being genuine, not too sales-y but also not dragging on the conversation for too long. At the end of the day, you are trying to get a meeting booked or make a sale. Don’t hide that fact.I go deeper into real world examples in my free newsletter which you can find here: https://bit.ly/3hbFI7Z#sales #outbound #prospecting

Product-Market-Fit (PMF) is not everythingInstead, most SaaS startups should be focused on SMF: Sales-Market-FitWhat do I mean with that? The focus on PMF means most companies take a narrow view. To have PMF or not to have PMF. Binary. And if the answer is no, they start changing the product.They should think more deeply about the sales motion instead, and assess whether there is Sales-Market-Fit (SMF). This is true especially for Enterprise-focused SaaS companies, where SMF can be viewed in 3 steps:👉 Getting to Yes - You pitch to a business user. You understood their pain. They even admit it. Then you explain your solution. How do they react? Do they stall, ask for more info, respond in a lukewarm way? Or are they like: YES! That's what I need! Where can I sign? The first step -is to get the user or buyer excited. Getting to "Yes, we need this." SMF1 unlocked.👉 Getting to PO - You might have SMF1, but that's not enough. Often, that's when it starts. The game. The sing & dance. With Procurement. With Security. IT. Compliance. The complex process of navigating all those internal stakeholders. Who don't have your sense of urgency. Who move slooooow. Or even block the deal completely. You'll have to have an 'Enterprise-ready' product. And you'll have to learn to navigate the process if you want to sign that Purchase Order (PO). When you show you can do that consistently & predictably, only then can you say you have SMF2. 👉 Getting to Revenue - PO done. ARR booked. But not ARR live. You can't recognise revenue yet. You can't send a bill. First, you have to onboard the customer, deliver the product. Making that as easy & fast as possible is not always easy. One founder I work with calls that "The Happy Path". Not just happy for the customer; also happy for you. Because recognising revenue today is happier than tomorrow. Getting to real Sales-Market-Fit is super hard. Large companies have dedicated teams and processes in place, often referred to as Quote-2-Cash (Q2C). Notice the focus on CASH. It's not just about selling, it's about getting to revenue and cash. You have to take an end-to-end approach: SMF 1, 2, and 3.Next time you discuss PMF with your team? Tell them about SMF Ps. How to become "more predictable" as a product team:* Work smaller* Work together* Work on less at once* Start finishing, stop starting* Regularly integrate your work* Get feedback "early and often"* Diverge at first* Allow the design to emerge* Focus on flow, adapt scope to time constraints* Commitment to continuous improvement and learning* Focus on support systems and tools* Celebrate wins, build in some down-time* Visualize the flow of work, bottlenecks, etc.* Don't worry about predictability. It is a byproductHow NOT to become "more predictable" as a product team:* Big-batches* Work in silos and individual projects* Pack quarters like a Tetris board* Start new things when you're blocked* Parallelize work, and integrate later* Treat your work as too precious to share* Converge quickly on a plan* Lock down designs upstream* Invest more time in estimation* Pressure teams to commit to deadlines* Dedicate all energy to "the project"* Ship and move on* Visualize work against a calendar* Worry about predictability. Make predictability the goal

📌 The most important and most challenging activity a Product Manager conducts regularly is backlog prioritization. Here are 5 popular frameworks with their pros, cons and use cases:𝟭) 𝗠𝗼𝗦𝗖𝗼𝗪: which stands for Must Have, Should Have, Could Have, and Would Like to Have. This framework allows product managers to prioritize features based on their necessity and level of importance. Pro: It's easy to understandCon: it does not consider the dependencies and the relative value of items. This framework is suitable for really simple products and projects.𝟮) 𝗞𝗮𝗻𝗼 𝗠𝗼𝗱𝗲𝗹: This is a framework for determining customer satisfaction with a product or service. It helps product managers to prioritize features based on their level of customer satisfaction and importance. Pro: it considers customer satisfactionCon: it does not take into account the cost and feasibility of a feature. This framework is suitable for customer-centric products and services.𝟯) 𝗩𝗮𝗹𝘂𝗲-𝗲𝗳𝗳𝗼𝗿𝘁: My framework of choice. This is a prioritization framework where you prioritize features based on their potential value and the effort required to implement them. It uses a 2-dimensional matrix to plot features on a grid.Pro: it allows to prioritize items based on the value and effort relative to each otherCon: the value and effort are often high-level guesses and therefore be totally off.I would say it's rather universal in its application𝟰) 𝗥𝗜𝗖𝗘: Here, one is prioritizing features based on their reach, impact, confidence, and effort. It helps you to prioritize features based on their potential reach and impact, as well as the level of confidence and effort required to implement them. Pro: it considers the potential reach and impact of features, Con: It's often hard to estimate the required parametersUse it only when you can reliably estimate those parameters, otherwise, it's a pure guessing game.𝟱) 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗪𝗲𝗶𝗴𝗵𝘁𝗲𝗱 𝗦𝗰𝗼𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗺𝗼𝗱𝗲𝗹: here each feature is evaluated against a set of criteria, such as customer value, feasibility, and alignment with the company's goals, and is assigned a score. The scores are then multiplied by the weight of each criterion to determine the overall priority of the feature. The features with the highest scores are considered to be the most important and should be prioritized.Positive: it allows one to prioritize against multiple vectors and adjust them based on the current strategynegative: it can be time-consuming and complex to set up and use effectively.This framework is suitable for products with many features and when multiple factors need to be considered while prioritizing.Of course, there are many other frameworks one can use. Which ones are your favourite? I really do hate the "The manager told me to do it!" one :) #productmanagement #productmanagers #prioritization

Seen some recent chat around Pre-Seed decks / packs 🛠️ A common structure I like 👇1. ~6 slide Teaser Deck (comfortable with this info being circulated outside of your control).2. ~10 slide Deck to send after a 1st call (the detail with DocSend access).3. DD Room on Notion or GDrive (post 2nd call and can add an evolving 'Investor Q&A' along with P&L, Incorp docs, product roadmap, tech stack analysis etc.).For the initial 6 Teaser Slides 👇1. OPENING 💥: 1 sentence on the business (if you had a billboard in Times Square, what would it say?).2. TEAM 🧙‍♀️🦸: Most important (obvs!). XP (not combined but individually). Only need Founder(s), not advisors and extended team. Worked together before? Show logos of previous companies. Finally, a sentence on why YOU will win. 3. PROBLEM 🤯: Simple & specific, highlighting the KEY market pain point & specific market size (for example, if it's Legaltech, don't highlight the entire market size of the legal sector).4. SOLUTION 🧠: The product. Show an image of the MVP, its simplicity & your insertion point. A couple of customer quotes (1 sentence is enough) is also cool if powerful.5. TRACTION 📈: 3 bullets on achievements you're proud of (Maybe a sexy chart). No tangible traction? Then talk about product progress, partners, killer hire etc. There will be something you are proud of! 6. THE ASK 💰: Raising X £/$/€. No need to highlight spend or overcomplicate. Just need to know how much you want to raise.Then go deeper in Deck 2 🙂

How can Product Managers leverage ChatGPT at work?Prepared this deck to showcase the variety of ways Product Managers can use the tool to generate and brainstorm ideas. ---Q: Why would a PM use an AI tool to auto-generate business models, strategies, personas & roadmaps without understanding/researching users first? A: Using ChatGPT as a first measure to figure out & finalize business models, roadmap, strategy etc. is counter-productive & irrational. That's not the point of this deck. No PM would ever make any meaningful progress without doing on-ground user discovery first.ChatGPT may help in pulling in public statistics, trends and literature with commentary (might not be accurate). Fueled with sufficient training data, it may also prove to be a highly useful aid. But that's not a replacement for primary research. Then, why are these use cases listed in the deck? To showcase how AI tools can provide assistance in the process and workflows. Ex: Let's take how a PM would use the tool for the business model canvas case. 1. SummarizationIf you train the model first with your customer intel and due diligence, ChatGPT can summarize your raw findings quickly into the canvas model. That "training" phase is an investment but the summary in the end saves time. 2. Sanity checksAfter a PM has put in efforts to research their user segment, they can ask ChatGPT for a different perspective to uncover any blind spots. Chatgpt's Canvas model may or may not be relevant to your case but it can help in spotlighting say, "cost structures", "revenue streams" or "partners" that you might have overlooked. It's a useful tool for increasing coverage. 3. BlueprintsSomeone uninitiated with business model canvas models can use ChatGPT to generate a quick snapshot for their particular use case. It can help them understand the kind of parameters they'll have to cater for. However, simply copy-pasting that into an investor presentation would be woefully wrong. The challenge/tradeoff is that doing this first can bias lines of thought. Q: ChatGPT isn't a knowledge model. How is any of this useful? Yes, I'm well aware that ChatGPT is a language model, and not necessarily a knowledge model. ChatGPT's using probability to guess the next word. But that that doesn't remove the fact that it's trained on a myriad of published documents. While it will fail to produce truly innovative solutions or ideas, it is capable of surfacing common thought patterns, list related concepts, spot check work against a body of work and act as a sparring partner. The attached doc assumes that PMs will use their better judgement to assess and use ChatGPT's output in meaningful ways. You've missed the point if you're hoping to reuse it's answers verbatim. #productmanagement #chatgpt

I've interviewed 100+ Product Managers as a hiring manager. With mass layoffs, many are applying for new roles. Here are 3 interview mistakes to avoid, applicable to 𝘢𝘯𝘺 𝘳𝘰𝘭𝘦 👇🌱 𝗡𝗼𝘁 𝘀𝗵𝗼𝘄𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘃𝘂𝗹𝗻𝗲𝗿𝗮𝗯𝗶𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆 A candidate that only speaks about their career wins and how great they are puts me off. We are all human and nobody is perfect. By not showing any vulnerability, I tend to see insecurity or overconfidence. Prepare to speak about what didn't work or where you are still learning. Show that you have a growth mindset.Make sure you frame it in a way that it's not something the employer needs to worry about. Practice with friends who can give an honest opinion when in doubt. Speaking about your failures and what you learned from them shows humility, low ego, and willingness to reflect. 💬 𝗡𝗼𝘁 𝗵𝗮𝘃𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗮 𝗰𝗼𝗻𝘃𝗲𝗿𝘀𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 Having a great conversation is not easy. Especially if it doesn't come naturally to you. The main mistake I've observed is candidates talking too much and not leaving space for the interviewer to speak. I've been in interviews where I was practically silent for 30 minutes after asking 1 question. What this tells me, is that someone is not interested in listening to others. Keep your answers short and to the point. Usually no more than a few minutes. When in doubt, check in with the interviewer; "Would you like me to elaborate more?". A candidate that is just answering questions briefly and letting silence fall in expectance of the next question is also not great. I find the best interviews are a two-way street. I love it when I get counter questions and an actual conversation starts to flow. 🦸 𝗡𝗼𝘁 𝗰𝗿𝗮𝗳𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗮𝗻 𝗶𝗻𝘀𝗽𝗶𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘀𝘁𝗼𝗿𝘆 At the beginning of the process, I ask PMs to give me a quick highlight reel of their career and anything else they might want to share. Many of them miss the opportunity here to tell a compelling story, while this is their chance to capture my interest. Storytelling is one of the most powerful tools to use. By creating a narrative that people can understand and can follow, you can drive your influence and positive impact on the business. Write down your life story in bullets. Make it short and to the point. Highlight the elements you want the employer to notice. Practice adding energy and enthusiasm to it.𝘛𝘩𝘢𝘵'𝘴 𝘪𝘵 𝘧𝘰𝘳 𝘯𝘰𝘸 🙏I would love for this post to help a few job seekers out there. If you liked it, please consider reposting it to your network so we increase the odds of that happening 🤞

I've a friend who charges $8k+ a month to turn your podcast into viral TikToks. So, I booked a meeting with him and stole all his tips. Sorry Eric. Here's some of the best advice I've ever gotten on repurposing 👇 ⚾ The purpose of the long form content is to take as many swings as possible to hit a home run. Create the long form podcast you want to make. But, create opportunities to spike attention within the content. These snippets are what you turn into shorts. As far as I understand, TikTok's can go viral regardless of the account they're posted on, or how well previous videos have done. we've all seen account with an average 2-3k views and one video that hit 6.8m views... So, you need to sprinkle in these soundbite opportunities. And we can do it with well placed questions. Work out how you can add hard hitting questions into your interviews. The questions with the highest chance of short, mind-blowing answers. 🤯 You learn how to ask these questions over time. It takes practice. Interviewing is a seriously difficult skill to master. Great questions to ask - 🤔 What was going through your head during....? 🤔 Where were you when you first knew things would change? What was that defining moment for you? 🤔 How did you know it would work out/fail/get to..... on time etc Try to avoid cliche questions. I like questions that result in the host laughing. In essence you want to give yourself ample swings. Same can be said for blogs. Writing great blogs gives your social team so much to work with. Making everyone's jobs easier. ---- I'm still super new to all this. I'm not at all the TikTok or Podcast guy. Just learning and sharing. 12 cold email copywriting rules that stack the odds in your favor for getting a response.1. Provide context.Why this person over other people? Research on social loafing tells us that people are more likely to take action when they feel uniquely qualified to do so.Example:“Josh, looks like you’re using Logic, Zubtitles and Headliner to edit and promote your podcast. Have you tried Descript?”2. Use emotionally charged language.“Does using spreadsheets to determine payouts feel like sipping peanut butter through a straw?” Emotions hit differently than facts and logic.3. Be crispy or specific.When you're crispy, you're more believable. The opposite is also true.“John, noticed you have a fleet of 23,500 in the EU and a goal of reaching zero emissions by 2025. DHL reduced fuel consumption by 10% across their grey fleet. Have you looked into in-cab feedback?”4. Keep it conversational.Formal: "I noticed your post on X, which triggered me to reach out."Conversational: "Your post on lowering resistance was spot on."5. Don’t worry too much about grammar.“Caught your take on X.”“Seems like you’re using Outreach.”“Scanned your jobs page and noticed ..”6. Distinguish what’s meaningfully different.Example for Descript:“Purge your recordings of "ums," "uhs", "you knows",with one click.”“Edit waveforms like docs.”7. Create a curiosity gap.People are hard-wired or close the gap between what they know and what they want to know. Ask a question that makes prospects think differently about their current solution.“Are you paying for subscriptions you forgot about?”“How do you know your sponge won’t scratch your car?”“Can your bike lock be picked with a paperclip in 15 seconds?”“Do 82% of your cold emails land in spam?”8. Never lie.9. Cut the fluff. Your email should fit on a mobile screen without scrolling.Original: “ACME was able to reduce customer hold times by 46%.Revision: “ACME reduced hold times by 46%."10. Reverse engineer cold emails that have received positive responses. Shameless plug - https://lnkd.in/e6Z3Y_YA 11. Steal words your customer use to describe the problem.How? Mine words between quotes in case studies. Your customers are better at writing cold emails than you are :-).Example:“Hard-pasting entire Excel pages into Google Sheets + manually make adjustments one sheet at a time to determine payouts?"12. Hire a writing assistant. Check out https://www.lavender.ai/

Fun fact: My worst performing LinkedIn posts drive the most revenue. Here’s why: 1. My “worst” performing posts aren’t because the content is bad. It’s because I’m sharing new thinking that isn’t yet widely accepted. These posts don’t get a lot of likes, but instead they challenge established thinking and spark curiosity. 2. Then I saw a post about Lavender and how it improves your reply rate through analytics/insights, so I bought it and started playing around with it. Lucky enough a month later, I met 💜📬 William M. Ballance & 💜🧙‍♂️ Josh Norris at a Demandbase hosted event during Dreamforce 2022.This sparked a more intense need to be better at cold emailing.🌔 Putting It Into Action: Early Q4 I started focusing my 1:1 conversations around emailing and how best to improve based on our messaging/products to our ICPs. I would send an email draft or sequence draft to my piers and managers to get feedback to then turn around and put it into Lavender. This is where the success starts coming in.By simply putting my emails into Lavender and following the suggestions and insights based on the Persona and aiming for a score of 90+, I knew my email reply rate would go up meaning my win rate for emails would go up as well.Pairing Lavender with business level personalization proved to be extremely successful. 🌕 End Results (TLDR): 80% of my Q3 quota attainment was from cold calling and social selling, where 20% was from cold emails.Fast forward to Q4 - I currently sit at around 40% of my Q4 attainment being from cold calls / social selling, where I now see ~60% coming from cold emailing w/ the help of Lavender.In conclusion, If you are struggling with cold emailing or it's an area where you'd like to improve, I highly suggest giving Lavender a try (they even have a trial!).
