A follow up, 14 more thoughts about life and business that I always plan to write more on, but never do.1) If you hate small talk, the easy solution to this is to do medium sized talk. Almost everyone likes it in the appropriate context. Don’t ask people went they arrived for a conference ask them what they are excited about. Don’t talk about the weather. Ask them about hobbies. Etc etc2) A huge skill is knowing what you don’t know, as is knowing what you think you know but don’t. Know when to seek a contrary honest opinion. In the words of Mark Twain , It ain't what you don't know that gets you into trouble. It's what you know for sure that just ain't so3) When you are not good at something it's quite hard to know when you should practice it more and try harder. or double down on what you do best, not do it and find someone else to do it. If people have tips on how to know, it would be great to hear them.4) We rarely think of how appreciative people are, but it's a key way to figure out people we may like working with or spending time with. And don't expect, appreciate. 5) People do have an aura. I don't care what science says, being with people who bring lightness and joy to the world is vital. Knowing what the energy you are putting out is, and knowing how to change it, changes every day.6) Offense takes two. People mistake whats offensive, and what it's possible to take offense from, the latter is something you can control.7)You are probably not tired. You are just not doing things that make you feel alive 8)Envy is useless. Focus entirely on your situation and life. Almost everything in life isn't zero sum. Others lives have essentially no impact on ours. 9)Algorithms will reinforce who you are, be careful of what they are doing to you. Social media is a mirror, it's never "its" fault, it's yours.10)You have a greater power to alters others' days than you think, consider the things you do that cost you nothing but impact others, our very presence is more responsibility than we readily assume.11)Most work is ass covering, if you build a culture of trust, admin and bureaucracy vanish and you speed up.12) Habits are compound interest to life. Most things in life are vicious circles or virtuous circles, be careful of the direction you are doing in but know turning it around makes everything better fast. Money makes money, power makes power, health creates health, it's scary13)An amazing way to think about your life is to give others’ advice, when do so, most advice you give will be stuff you should be acting on yourself 14) Uncertainty - means anything can HAPPEN, but it also means ANYTHING CAN happen, use it to your advantage.BONUS, the best way to start doing something is to start doing something. SOUNDS SO DUMB, but most people never ever ever start. In order to write a book, write a tweet, then an articleSorry if this sounded a bit like some cheesy poster in the Sale bin at TKMaxx. Live love laugh. :)

How to do a hard reset on your life (in 30 minutes):This is a process I run through when life gets a bit too messy. Like when you're constantly thinking back to a time when "life was better." Or when your routines fall apart and all of these little tasks pop up that drain all of your time in the day. 1) Take Note Of How You Feel Pull out a notebook and write down: - Exactly what you are doing on a daily basis.- How you feel morning, afternoon, and night. Take 10 minutes and get specific. This is important for identifying unconscious parasites (time and energy suckers). 2) Prioritize, Remove, & Restructure The reason you aren't getting the results you used to is because: - You feel pressed for time- You don't have energy to get results- You stopped doing the things that got results Look over your brain dump from above. What can you change? Prioritize the things that will get results. Remove the things that snuck their way into your day (and don't deserve to be there). Restructure specific tasks and obligations to free up more time. Get the chaotic structure of your mind on paper and reorganize it. 3) Create A Week Long Plan You are in this chaotic spot in life because of entropy. The organization of your life (and therefore the mind) tends toward disorder if you don't perform mental housekeeping. This is dangerous, because you can pick up bad habits along the way. Habits are difficult to break. But you can make the process easier with clarity. Take 10 minutes. Write out every single thing you are going to do for the next week. This shouldn't sound crazy or difficult. This is how you reduce the friction of making better decisions. Write out: - Your morning routine- Your focused work routine- Other tasks and meetings- Nightly routine You will have to experiment as you go. If the routine doesn't flow, go back to your notebook and write what you're going to do the next day. This is how you create a system for your life. It gets more efficient with time if you make this 'mental housekeeping' a regular practice.If you enjoyed this, leave a comment with your plans for 2023.Curious to see what you all are building.

Booking.com runs 25,000 A/B tests annually – here is how: 👇 In 2017 Stuart Frisby has shared 10 amazing principles that demonstrate how Booking.com has integrated experimentation into its core business strategy for growth. Let's dive in…➡️ 1. If it can be a test, test it.At Booking, we test everything that can be tested in order to improve performance and introduce new features. If something cannot be tested, it is unlikely to be implemented.➡️ 2. No platform goes untested.We make sure that every platform we use undergoes thorough testing.➡️ 3. No HIPPOs.We believe in democratizing testing and avoiding the influence of "HIPPOs" (highest paid person's opinion) in experimentation.➡️ 4. Teams are made for testing. Our teams are designed to be able to fully test hypotheses independently, without dependencies or bottlenecks on other teams or parts of the business. Each team member supports the testing of hypotheses.➡️ 5. Everyone gets 100% access.Everyone has access to all the information they need to develop valuable hypotheses.➡️ 6. Hypotheses > ideas.We prioritize testing hypotheses over simply generating ideas, as hypotheses are grounded in reality and require proof or disproof, whereas ideas do not. We believe that those who come up with hypotheses are designers, whereas those who come up with ideas are artists.➡️ 7. Test Small.We test small to limit risk and investment. By testing small, atomic experiments, we can make small investments and better understand what works and what doesn't.➡️ 8. Guidelines > Rules.We have guidelines for testing, but we also encourage creativity and flexibility in experimentation. We recognize that AB testing can be difficult and requires both a supportive culture and knowledge to be successful.➡️ 9. 9/10 tests fail.We understand that many tests will fail - often as much as 9 out of 10. As we continue to test, we may find that the low-hanging fruit is no longer within reach, and even well-conceived tests can fail for various reasons. However, we believe that the research and learning gained from these failed tests is valuable.➡️ 10. Customers drive the product.We believe that the best way to drive product development is through small, incremental steps that can be measured and informed by customer feedback.Highly recommend to watch the video: https://lnkd.in/eT8Zj8ma

I only know 2 VPs of Sales that ever went from $0 to $250M ARR. One of them is Mark Kosoglow from Outreach & I knew I wanted him for our CRO role. Here’s how I recruited him, play-by-play 👇1️⃣ Nobody great is looking to leave right their job immediately & almost always say no to new opportunities. To combat this, use the ‘advisor’ tactic. Equity in exchange for advice. De-risk getting to know them and helps you both get to ‘try’ before you buy and it will warm them up to the opportunity over time. 2️⃣ I set him up with our top investor from Accel to have 1:1 *casual* conversations. Casual is key. They can’t feel you are full-court press, even if you are. You must lure them in slowly. 🎣3️⃣ If you’ve met Mark you know he thrives in energy. Remember the scene from WeCrashed with Adam Neumann & how he set up the “perfect” office environment, decided where people sat, what it looked like, & what the energy *felt* like for Masayoshi from SoftBank when he entered?I ran that play. Made sure the office was packed. Made sure every SDR & AE was in there hitting the phones to give him the memories of Outreach in the early days which he mentioned to me he missed. I remember watching him from the corner of my desk; it was like a kid in Disneyland. Big smile, ear to ear. He was getting hooked. 😅4️⃣ Mark told me he wanted to be back in the boardroom. At Outreach the company got too big & they didn’t need him in there anymore. So you know what I did? I faked a board meeting just for him & had him come out to NY for it. He still actually has no clue… until he reads this post.Myself, my co-founder, & the board was there ready to make sure he felt like he was a part of the team already. If Scorsese wrote a movie on recruiting, this scene was it, and I made Mark the protagonist. 🍿5️⃣ After the “board meeting” went as planned, I took him to dinner with Edward and I. Blockchain is NOT web3.Neither are all the cryptocurrencies and tokens.Here's a simple way to think about it:The base layer - BlockchainThe value layer - CryptocurrencyThe real world/crypto bridge - OraclesThe application layer - dApps The governance layer - DAOs The "keys" to your crypto - Self-Custodial WalletsThe identity layer - NFTsIf you combine all these, you get web3.Anything else you would add?Let me know in the comments. #web3 #crypto #blockchain #cryptocurrency #NFT

How do we run our stand-up? A person shares the board and we simply ask "do you need anything today?"Team members will either say 'no, I am good' or a variation of - I need a decision on this...- I need someone to check this...- Can someone help with this...- FYI everyone should be aware we are doing this...Now, when new team members join they are normally uncomfortable that we don't discuss specific tickets. It feels very alien to them at first and that is understandable. They can feel a bit lost and uninformed. However, if you trust your team and you believe everyone is aligned then there is nothing more that you need. Even with super-large teams of 20-25 people the stand-up will take less than 15 minutes.Other questions at stand-up such as 'what are you working on or what did you do yesterday' are indicative of an anti-pattern somewhere else in the team or organisation. With frequent repetition of the vision & roadmap the newer team members will easily gain total awareness. Henry David Thoreau said "It is not what you look at that matters, it is what you see". Stop looking at the tickets and start seeing what people need day to day. Edit: Oh we also don't make people actually stand-up. It's archaic.#Scrum #Agile #Project #StandUp

Hosting 30 Minutes to President's Club has changed me.I sell completely differently from when I started.Some things I've learned:Remove friction from the sale. Withholding pricing/demo until you do "discovery" frustrates buyers.Admitting your product isn't perfect builds massive trust.Ask "So that" questions. "How are you doing X so that Y does not happen?" (X is a process you make easier; Y is a negative impact of not solving)The Humbling Disclaimer ("I feel a little awkward asking this...") softens any tough question.Tie personalization to the business problem you solve (Calling out someone went to USC then immediately asking if they want to buy lead gen software isn't it.)Drop the excessive formality with executives. You don't have to address emails "Dear Mr. Cegelski..."Block 5 minutes at the end of your meetings to set next steps. Don't try to cram this into the last 30 seconds.Quit messing around with social touches & crafting perfect emails. Time block 1 hour and do nothing but cold call for that hour.Stop focusing on HOW the buyer is doing X. Instead, focus on the business problem and solve that.Multithread like a madman. Have 1:1 conversations with everyone evaluating your thing.

Be careful. Most "products" are, in fact, projects. 9 red flags (and how it should work):1. You start your initiative by writing a large PRD.2. Your goal is to implement the requirements (features).3. You have an Analyst who collects them all in the "initial phase."4. You have a time-based, feature-based roadmap.5. People see no need to test the ideas before implementing them.6. There is no UX Designer on the team.7. There are no product analytics. You don't really know how people are using your product.8. There is no product strategy. You try to maximize sales by satisfying all customers.9. You work in a customer-vendor relationship (internal or external).-The desired characteristics:1. Your cross-functional team is empowered to solve the problems.2. PM, Designer, and Engineers perform Product Discovery together. Continuously.3. You have an outcome-based roadmap. Preferably Now-Next-Later.4. If you commit to a date, you do it rarely and only after the Discovery. You never commit too early (Marty Cagan's advice).5. You manage the value, usability, feasibility, and viability risks.6. The riskiest assumptions are tested before the implementation by running experiments.7. Choosing, instrumenting, and tracking the right metrics is key.8. You ship incrementally, measure the outcomes and learn from it.9. Tradeoffs are essential. What you do, but also what you don't. You respect your market and the unique value proposition.-The time before the launch might be a bit different:1. You must define the market, unique value proposition, business model, and initial vision and strategy.2. You should test your business idea with the help of the MVP prototype before the implementation.3. You need to define the go-to-market strategy.4. You can't rely on product analytics before the product is launched, so you will rely more on customer interviews. Still, there are ways to collect behavioral, quantitative data even in the initial phase (prototypes like the Landing Page MVP and behavioral surveys).5. Product Discovery should be performed by the Product Trio. Nothing changes here, in my opinion.-Hope that helps. What are your thoughts?

Product Managers need to spend time understanding user problems and framing them in a way that sets strong constraints for the team. Many PMs jump into solution-finding before they truly understand the problem. This leads to ineffective solutions, or indecisiveness as their teams struggle to eliminate potential solutions. What happened to Agile? I feel like we failed it.The idea was beautiful, but what I see is quite ugly.No matter the framework teams use, I often observe more or less the same, that hurts me because we can do better than that:❌ Output over Outcome❌ Opinions over Evidence❌ Plans over Goals❌ Control over EmpowermentAt first, the framework will get the blame because we think the “process” is getting in the way. Here are common complaints:😫 Scrum sucks! Sprints get in the way of creating useful software🧐 Kanban is better than Scrum because we don’t waste time with pointless events😰 We cannot set Sprint Goals because we have too many important items to deliver next Sprint🤯 Our Product Owners cannot make decisions alone. I don’t know why we need themSorry to break it to you. You’re missing the point when that happens.Frameworks are just means. The objective isn’t to master Scrum, Kanban, or whatever else. The goal is to create value faster.What I often observe is a fixed mindset instead of a growth one. This ensures all frameworks get you more or less to the same place, nowhere.🚀 If we want to have a chance of creating value faster than we’re used to, we better adapt our mindset. We’ve got to:✅ Move from plans to embrace the unknown✅ Move from command and control to empowerment✅ Move from opinions to evidence✅ Move from increasing predictability to accelerating learnsFirst things first. What do you think about it? Is the problem the vehicle or the driver?Read the full article to know more about it - link in the comments.Let’s rock the product world.#productmanagement #agile #productowner #agilecoach #scrummaster #productmanager

Heard from two CFOs yesterday. They've become the key stakeholder in all tech purchases. Here's how to sell to them:😑 Sitting on the shelf is their biggest fear. Tech that’s purchased but not adopted is a nightmare. Change management and end user experience is key. Sellers need to give confidence in the plan to make this as seamless as possible. 🤲 Simplicity in a business case is key. An ROI for a CFO should be no more than two pages. Don’t include any indirect savings in an ROI (ie, “productivity gains”). Include direct savings for the software that is replacing something else. ⏳ It’s not just about price, it’s also about timing. Most CFOs are focused on ways to start small (free trial, paid pilot) and then have their FP&A teams write up an ROI analysis for the larger deal later. 😎 Make the business case easy to resell. In many cases, CFOs have to sell your business case to their board. You don’t just have to deliver a solid business case, you also have to do the buyer enablement to make it easy for them to resell. 🤝 References are critical. They want to know why their peers are using it and why they love it. Be proactive in setting up these references, don’t wait to be asked. ——— Want more on this topic? Since the #recession, I've had 233 VPs of Sales, CROs, VPs of CS, and CCOs DM me how to grow more efficiently. 5 ideas I share:⛔️ These ideas are risky, innovative, and require work. They aren't easy so if you only do "safe," stop reading now. ⛔️ 1. Lessen focus on acquisition - deals are harder, there's more scrutiny, grumpy CFOs ruin deals, indecision runs rampant. When the going gets hard, the hard get going...but the smart find an easier way to win.🤪 Crazy fix - Lower new biz quotas to make attainment believable and build momentum. People don't sell more bc their quotas are higher (😱), they sell more when they are winning. It's OK to pay more for that revenue - it's harder to win now.2. Increase focus on expansion - customers are the easiest, fastest, cheapest, and least risky way to grow. 🤪 Crazy fix - Make multiple roles responsible for expansion. The time to your 2nd deal is the greatest predictor of revenue growth, so keep AEs on the account to land a 2nd deal within 60 days. Then, AMs can run their normal motion while you make CSMs pipe gen machines by SPIFFing CSQL creation. #teamwork3. Lower your initial land expectations - AEs wanna land big. Cool, except the world rewards NRR, and if you run the account dry up front, the downside exposure is high. Even if the revenue ends up the same, investors punish you for "how" you got it.🤪 Crazy fix - Measure "Expansion Inventory." This is the amount of seats or revenue that you can expand into after the initial land. Prioritize accounts with readily accessible expansion inventory rather than 1st deal size. Do what it takes to win them (even discounts). These accounts are your NRR ringers and create data that says you can grow efficiently.4. Onboarding is the new revenue driver - Get 'em on, make 'em happy, and they'll reward you. Do that fast and in a way that's easy for the customer, and revenue will go up.🤪 Crazy fix - Instead of spending time raging on AEs and AMs for prospecting, have them collaborate with Implementation Managers and CSMs to build an express onboarding plan (aim to speed up by 2X). Don't stop the plan at CSM handoff...take it all the way to 2nd deal. This will be the best thing you do for #NRR and win rates this year.5. Re-orient CS away from retention - Focusing CS on retention is like focusing a marathoner on mile marker 26.2...that's too far out to feel attainable. Successful marathoners break the race into chunks and win each chunk, letting a series of winnable races result in a victory 26.2 miles later.🤪 Crazy fix - Architect Moments of Impact...lots of them. Map out how to create them, then link them to customer initiatives and outcomes and set CSMs loose. They'll be happier and so will your customers.

Hot Take: Your product team doesn’t need a “North Star Metric”, it needs a "North Star Strategy". There are two camps of thinking. On one side, some product leaders believe strongly in the North Star Metric approach. They value the clarity and objectivity that comes from focusing the team’s efforts on a single metric.Amplitude's North Star Playbook defines the approach:“A team using the North Star Framework identifies a single, meaningful metric and a handful of contributing inputs. Product teams work to influence those inputs, which in turn drive the metric. The North Star is a leading indicator of sustainable growth and acts as a connective tissue between the product and the broader business.”I’m in the other camp... I think it’s difficult and sometimes misguided to try to channel an entire team’s efforts into a single metric. The North Star Metric approach suffers from a few problems.First, it assumes that everything valuable a team can do ladders up to the same metric — which provides an inflexible way of thinking about how to deliver value to the customer.Second, it suffers from Goodhart’s Law: when a measure becomes a target, it ceases to become a good measure. People optimize for the metric, rather than the value it’s supposed to represent.Instead, it’s important for the team to express their goals as a strategic narrative — what’s the couple of sentences that describe what we’re trying to achieve and why?From there, the team can define the handful of metrics that are the evidence that the team is making progress on that strategic narrative. This approach focuses on the strategic thinking, not just the number — and it gives the team the responsibility and flexibility of solving for the true nature of the problem, not just hitting a quantitative goal.Tinder went through a North Star Metric exercise: what one metric should the product team use to evaluate impact? At first, matches seemed like the right metric. But, there are good matches (that result in conversations) and bad matches (that never result in conversations).Maybe active conversations? This is a good metric for some teams, but it’s a terrible metric for the trust & safety team -- scammers are very good at starting conversations when left unchecked.We searched some more. By the time we were done, our ideal North Star Metric was so generic that it no longer served any useful purpose for the guiding the team.Tinder has a very clear North Star Strategy -- make single life more fun by helping people match and talk with the people they want to meet. But, there is no single metric that can measure that strategy. Each team needs to define their work relative to that strategy, and each team needs different metrics to measure that progress.We needed to ladder up to a single strategy, not a single metric. We needed to align our efforts with logic, not math.What's the best way to end a hot take? With a flame war! Tell me what I got right or wrong in the comments.

Do you know what’s killing product management? Bullshit management 😖 It doesn’t matter which industry you’re in; BS gets in the way and distracts you from doing what really creates value. Companies may hire you as a Product Manager and quickly limit your ability to create value. You must be extra careful with bullshit management because it wastes all your energy. ❌ I define Bullshit Management as the art of spending your time doing things that leads to anything but creating value. ✅ While Product Management is the art of uncovering opportunities to create value sooner. ❌ BS Management gets in the way of PM, and creating value becomes just a dream. Here are some signs you’re not doing product management: ❌ Gathering requirements from stakeholders to solve their wants instead of establishing relationships with them to deliver on end-users needs. ❌ Keeping an extensive Product Backlog just to tell stakeholders their requests are registered instead of removing items unrelated to your current goals. ❌ Preparing frequent performance reports for management instead of evaluating the impact of your outputs. ❌ Striving for consensus to please all stakeholders instead of seeking the best option for the product. ❌ Signing off all items delivered by the team to ensure it matches your quality control instead of empowering them to reach goals. ❌ Attending several meetings to avoid pissing off critical stakeholders instead of mindfully investing your time into what matters. ❌ Fear of saying no to pointless requests because your boss may get an e-mail instead of focusing on what contributes to your goals. ❌ Bridging communication between business stakeholders and developers instead of being a catalyst and fostering collaboration. ❌ Prioritizing features based on opinions instead of learning from end-users. ❌ Focusing on delivering features over maximizing the value. ❌ Spending time explaining why an initiative failed instead of sharing what you learned from your failure. ✅ Good product management is about having a vision, testing many small ideas with little funds, and gradually increasing them as you get objective evidence. ✅ The goal is to create value as fast as possible, not to give pointless predictability. Embrace the unknown to uncover hidden opportunities. Everyone faces bullshit management at some point in time. You cannot avoid it, but you can deal with it in a way that creates value. Never send a cold email asking for a meeting.The first time you engage with someone should be focused on building the relationship, not booking a meeting. Building a relationship is like building a bank account. You start at zero. You make deposits to that bank account and eventually, there's enough in the account to take withdrawals. Make too many withdrawals and the account will be overdrawn.It's the same with prospecting.Asking for a meeting in a cold email is like trying to withdraw money from an empty bank account. So how do you make deposits in this "Emotional Bank Account" to build a relationship with your prospect? According to Stephen Covey, there are 6 ways to make deposits:1 Understanding the Individual2 Keeping Commitments3 Clarifying Expectations4 Attending to the Little Things5 Showing Personal Integrity6 Apologizing When We Make a WithdrawalLet's look at how this translates to your prospect.1. Understanding the Individual - we are all busy and no one wants another useless meeting on their calendar. Seek first to understand their challenge.An excellent example of a cold email that seeks first to understand:"Hey Maria! I saw that you are a Hubspot partner but was curious if you support Hubspot exclusively or support other CRMs as well?"2. Keeping Commitments - if you tell the prospect you will follow up in a week, you'd better follow up in a week. Not keeping your commitment to follow up is a withdrawal from the bank account with low funds already.3. Clarifying Expectations - Your prospect is not a mind reader. Don't expect them to know what you expect of them. A relationship is successful when all parties know what is expected of them. 4. Attending to the Little Things - You cannot build a relationship without investing time in getting to know the person. Start by doing a little research and personalizing the email. Small deposits in the beginning can have compounding effects.5. Showing Personal Integrity - Let your words and actions be a reflection of your moral values. Don't mislead your prospect.6. Apologizing When We Make a Withdrawal - this doesn't mean starting your email with "Hey sorry for bothering you". This means when you violate any of the previous 5 principles, you own it. You apologize for it. What else would you add to this list? How do you build uniquely better relationships with your prospects?#salesenablement #coldemail #outreach #prospecting #demandgeneration #email #revenue #sales

What's your goal when cold-calling someone?Mine used to be to book in a meeting.Which is a lot of pressure when I'm calling someone who has probably never heard of me or the company I work for.This mindset of focusing on a big outcome meant I was nervous whenever dialing.So you can probably guess how those calls went...But over time I realised the goal of my calls wasn't to book a meeting but in fact to gain a little bit of information.That information could be around the competitor they're using or even the right person to be reaching out to.Because those nuggets I find out can be used in future outreach to make my messaging stronger.So, as an example, if I found out they use a certain competitor my next call might sound like this...."Hey Billybob, spoke to Lizzie the other day do you have a second? She mentioned you're using Competitor X and from your recent funding I can see you're expanding into the US which is exciting. Usually when speaking to companies who are scaling globally, like you are, will run into the same two challenges when using Competitor X.A & B.Wondering which of these is most real in your world?"And again the goal is to find information.So if Billbob tells me A is an issue but not interested in another call I can then use that extra nugget to make my next outreach to them or another stakeholder even more relevant.And as soon as I stopped focusing on booking a meeting and started focusing on finding useful information, I stopped being so nervous.And the funny thing is, from not sounding so desperate & pushy I would book more meetings as a result.What's your goal when cold-calling?Want more of my tips & tricks then you can check out my Prospect Attractors guide here:https://lnkd.in/ewGHYW5w#sales

The phrase “personal branding" feels kind of icky.Remember school projects? There was always that one kid who didn’t do much work, but was great at talking. Of course the teacher thought they did all the work. Personal branding reminds me of that person, now all grown up–and still talking their way through life.Most of us are not good at “personal branding.” A lot of good people are too busy actually doing the work to manage the optics of that work.Unfortunately, this means well-deserving people often get the least credit.Let’s stop aiming for “personal branding,” and start aiming for “personal credibility.”How are the two different?Personal branding has a superficial undertone. It assumes you have your work, then you tack on an artificial layer of “branding” to shape perceptions. It doesn’t respect the intelligence of your audience because it assumes people will believe whatever brand you put out there. Personal credibility is about substance first and foremost. Then showing the folks around you what you can do and how you can contribute. It’s about earning trust in order to do more of the work you want to do. When you have a strong personal brand, you might get likes on your social media posts. But when you have strong personal credibility, you have a deeper connection with people who believe in your work. This means you have more options, more control, and a more fulfilling career. How do you build your personal credibility? By doing the same things that help you believe other people (or products) are credible. Social proof, accolades, a track record of contribution, good design, strong writing, being articulate, testimonials, work samples, having a strong point of view, warm referrals. All the things that make you think, “Hmm this person seems like they know their craft.” There are lots of people who don’t have strong personal brands, but have strong personal credibility. When you Google them, you might not find a lot. But they have plenty of interesting opportunities in the circles and networks they belong in.And that counts for a lot.PS Some of the most credible experts are already teaching on Maven. If you want to build your own course, check out the Maven Course Accelerator. It’s free: http://bit.ly/3gk0tOV

When you start managing someone, use your 1:1s in the first couple of weeks to understand their Context. Most new managers focus solely on getting up to speed on Content (projects, goals, milestones, blockers, etc.)But by balancing Context & Content, you can help move the relationship much faster from Introduction to Rapport to Credibility to Trust.Example 1:1 questions1) Tell me your career story2) What is working well for you here3) What is not working well for you 4) What type of work energizes you most5) What type of work depletes your energy6) How do you prefer to receive feedback7) What forms of recognition do you prefer the most / the least8) Tell me about your relationship with key team members9) What is the most pressing issue I can assist you withNote that it takes most people some time to figure out an authentic answer to some of these questions. But by asking these questions as you are starting to work with them, you get the dialogue started, you activate self-inquiry around these topics, and most importantly, you remove any awkwardness around broaching these topics later on in the relationship.

There are two main types of effective cold email. The best practices are similar, but there are important differences: 1️⃣ Super short, persona based. This is the type of email you see the most advice about. The best practices are: ✓ Short subject line✓ 50ish words✓ Resonance w/ persona’s problem✓ Hint at solution✓ Interest-based call-to-actionThis style is useful for all sorts of personas and levels of seniority. ——— 2️⃣ Hyper-personalized, person based. These are less popular, since they take much more effort and are more difficult to do “at scale.” Don’t let that stop you, though! You don’t need to pump out thousands of these to be successful. The best practices are: ✓ Short subject line✓ 125ish words✓ Personalized to recipient✓ Segue from research to value prop✓ Hint at solution✓ Interest-based call-to-actionThis style is typically reserved for “above the line” decision makers since they take longer to craft. ——— 💪 To be successful, I strongly believe you need a healthy mixture of both. #sdr #sales #revenue

I run a 100% remote team. And I *hate* micromanagement. Here’s how our team stays accountable: Self-reporting in Slack. It’s that simple. 🤷🏻At the end of the day, everyone shares what they got done. And it works. Our account managers run 150 accounts each. They handle onboarding, calls, and retention. It’s a lot to juggle. But with this system, each employee “owns” their corner of the company. We trust them to get their work done. And they crush it.Too many employers are worried about their employees slacking off. So they try to “keep people accountable” using invasive management techniques. The reality? Your employees are adults too. Set up a system that allows them to report like adults. Then get out of their way.

I get the feeling we are going about Sustainability wrong.It seems to me that it's largely being "solved for" in a superficial way. It's being driven by optics and reputation, not from a deep seated belief in doing the right thing.Its being considered a problem to react to quickly, and defensively, not as an opportunity, one that needs to be properly understood, and proactively and enthusiastically and comprehensively tackled.My fear is the next few years will see companies solve it at the "communications" layer. Instead of going deeper to the core values of the company or to the products and services made, or to supply chains, companies will simply make surface level changes, of little consequence and spend millions Advertising whats been done.Greenwashing is already here and it threatens to undermine efforts being made. Five years ago Cisco Systems vowed to slash 60% of its operations-related emissions by 2022 from 2007 levels, and it made a proud announcement recently that it had gone even further, and reduced them by 61%. Yet all it did was spend $600,000 on buying Carbon Credits, something widely thought to have literally no impact on the planet whatsoever. By any normal market accounting for emissions they've actually gone up 22% in the time period. All around me I see superficial signs, if IKEA wanted to be sustainable, perhaps it should focus on making more expensive furniture that lasts far longer and act as a reseller for items.If Zara wants to get press coverage for launching a resellers platform, great, but this is the same company that has driven fast fashion for years, putting expendability into the minds of consumers. Why not focus on making more expensive clothes, on movements towards timeless fashion, and making products that actually are the size they are supposed to be. The world would be far better placed if we solved problems more deeply, the solution to gun control doesn't start with ads saying guns are bad, it starts with deep questioning of the problem so solutions can be found at other levels.

I spent over 16,000 hours working in PM.And made hundreds of mistakes. Top 8 things I wish I had known before I started:1. Control doesn't workThe more you tighten the control, the more you need it. It can only result in people being disengaged at work.Instead of giving precise instructions, co-create goals, provide support, and hold teams accountable for the outcomes.See "I won't lie. I wasted 3 years of my PM career" under my profile ("featured").2. Hiring is essentialHiring is one of the most important jobs of a manager. Don't be pressured by time or important initiatives for which you need experts.When in doubt, keep looking. In particular, never hire an a**hole. 3. Be careful when doing business with friendsI'm not saying it's impossible. But before doing business with your friend, ask yourself: What can go wrong? Am I ready to sacrifice business for friendship?4. Scrum\Agile certificates are uselessCertificates are useless in general. On top of that, Agile is about 15% of the PM's work. Learn PM instead. Every day try to wake up 1 hour earlier to read books or listen to podcasts. You can also do it on public transport, in a car, or when training at the gym.If that's too much, start with 2 hours a week. 1 year = 104 hours of learning.5. Most "products" are projectsAt least 80% of the PO and PM jobs are "backlog administrators" or "project managers."Instead of focusing only on cracking the PM interview, invest some time in understanding what good PM is and compare it with how the company works. Ensure your new manager will have time to coach you and help you grow. This is essential, especially if you are starting your career.6. Test new business ideasNever ask people what they think about your business idea. Positive opinions and words of support are useless.You need to validate whether people are ready to send you their money. Test your ideas with the help of MVP prototypes.Learn about Jobs-to-be-Done and Outcome-Driven Innovation.7. Discover what to build nextMost of your ideas are not going to work. Thus, learning by delivering is expensive.Apply Continuous Product Discovery and work in a Product Trio to identify opportunities, ideate solutions, and validate the riskiest assumptions.And do not let your customers design solutions. It's not their job.8. Take care of yourself first Take care of your mental and physical health. Do not work over hours. Do physical activity. Think about your diet. Sleep 7-8 hours. And find time for your family and friends.Take care of yourself first. Then think about your work and career.TL;DR1. Control doesn't work2. Hiring is essential3. Be careful when doing business with friends4. Scrum\Agile certificates are useless5. Most "products" are projects6. Test new business ideas7. Discover what to build next8. Take care of yourself first Do you agree? Disagree? I'm still learning and don't know everything. I'm happy to hear your opinion.

GREAT storytelling is not easy. That’s why there are so many “coaches” on Linkedin, sharing complicated structures, methodologies and frameworks. But it all comes down to just 11 words.And when you’re time short and under pressure to deliver something compelling, who doesn’t want some quick prompts to help you tell a better story?🧠 WHAT IF….?Disney start new story workshops exploring ideas for future scripts with just 2 words. “What if….”. Not only do these words spark the neurons in your brain to think differently, but they challenge you to surprise the audience with something they’ve not seen before. The more ideas the better. Get the obvious ones out of the way first.Travis Kalanick used this method to create Uber - “What if you could push 1 button and a taxi arrived to pick you up in under 120 seconds?”🚧 INTENTION & OBSTACLEAt its core, every story is a journey where somebody (or something) is transformed. People talk about Aristotle’s Poetics, Save the Cat or Joseph Campbell’s “Hero with a thousand faces” but it all comes down to intention and obstacle.Somebody wants something REALLY badly. And something is standing in their way of getting it. The stronger the intention, and the the obstacle (or villain) standing in their way, the better the story will be. In business, your hero could be an executive, an audience, a customer or a product. BUT until you’ve introduced a BIG obstacle, you don’t have a story. Think internal politics, market forces, competition, lack of innovation….⏰ THE 3 C’sDan Brown describes his process for writing thrillers like Da Vinci Code as coming down to the 3 C's.CONTRACT: Tell your audience exactly what is about to happen and paint an exciting picture of the journey they’re about to go on. Assure them that if they stick with you, it will have a dramatic ending that they didn’t expect, which will transform them for the better.CLOCK: Every good thriller is more compelling when there's a sense of urgency. The world might not explode if Robert Langdon doesn’t find the antidote by midnight, but there may be consequences of inaction, failure or financial loss for the business story you are telling. CRUCIBLE: There is a boundary, where every story takes place. Stay within that boundary. Don’t introduce parts of a different story that don't need to be there. What you leave out is more important than what you put in.😲 “AND THEN WHAT HAPPENED?”If your audience is thinking these 4 words, then you’re on the right path. Forget about all the science (dopamine?), structures and systems, just do everything in your power to get your audience on the edge of their seat wondering where you are going to take the audience next.For your next presentation, ask yourself “HOW can I surprise THIS audience and give them something they don’t expect?”Keep these 11 words up your sleeve the next time you need to tell a story and you won't go far wrong.

I've invested in 102 companies (~$9M deployed) in the last 2 years. I've probably heard more than 1000 start pitches. One thing stood out - most people suck at explaining what they do. These are smart people. Hard-working. Building interesting projects, night and day.​Yet they can’t explain their business in 30 seconds in a way that is:• Clear• Interesting.​I call this painting a picture while tickling the brain. An easy solution: the Storybrand method.​It forces you to explain your business in simple terms:• Most people have this problem• We offer a simple solution• So that you get a this happy ending​Let's take this example: BEFORE:We re-locate manufacturing operations from china to mexico. So many benefits - Mexico can do it better and cheaper than china.​^ not bad, the audience rated it a 5.5 out of 10. This is about average.​​AFTER:Most brands are having manufacturing problems in China right now due to the Coronavirus.​We, at GetAyuda, find you a factory in Mexico who can match the quality and cost of China.​So you can stay focused on selling, while we’re working 24/7 to find you a backup supplier in Mexico. Or another example: BEFORE:​Sophya provides virtual spaces to become the future of work. Because we’re all losing our sense of community and connectedness. So anyone, wherever you work, can get together and re-discover the way we used to work.​^ lots of jargon. Virtual spaces? Future of work? Rediscover the way we used to work? Huh? Not clear.​AFTER: ​Most remote workers miss bumping into their coworkers every day in the snack room or hallways.​We create a virtual office, where you can walk around and bump into your coworkers casually, almost like a video game.​So that people can have fun talking to their coworkers again, not just scheduled zoom meetings.​^Not perfect, but much better than jargonpalooza he had 5 minutes earlier. I'll share some more resources in the comments below. Storytelling takes practice and is a valuable skill they don't teach you in school. Someone could make a lot of $$$ creating the best-in-class content on telling stories.

Maybe we should be drastic, not agile.“Move fast and break things”, “Test and Learn”, “Dynamic optimization”, “Fail fast”, we’ve all embraced the thinking of the modern age and it's to be reactive, to try things, to respond fast to always be changing. We've a contemporary idea that strategy is forged and refined by A-B testing, product market fit comes from a low cost trial, we can make MVP's and see what the market thinks. Shein sells 150,000 different items per year and just reorders more of what seems to work. It's a business rooted in data not vision, nor belief. Clearly moving fast is good, clearly bureaucracy is bad, but sometimes I’m not sure we're able to know when to be fast and when to be stubborn. When to ponder deeply and stick to our guts and when to pivot fast as the wind changes. When to make the market work around us, and when to work around it. A tiny example. Can anyone remember the last software update that changed things that much?Maybe it was 2013 and iOS 7.0, where we shifted from Skuemorphic design to flat more iconic design. I'm not complaining, it's just thousands of people have for years worked their buts off, spending billions of dollars, to release 7 to 10 new versions of iOS every year, of which nobody notices much difference. Of course, over several years it's improved.Can you imagine the extraordinary work that goes into testing, compliance, security patches, more testing, bug fixing, and more. And now we've software thats more bloated, slower and more complex than ever before.It's the same for most companies, if in 2010 Apple and Microsoft or Sony or Samsung or Sonos, or Adobe had said, every 5 years we will make something extraordinarily bold and new and different. We'd have software that's transformative, not iterative.  Car makers do this well. TV makers do not. Most companies today are in a fight trying to keep up with today, they are endlessly trying to keep as far behind others as they are. They are tweaking. I wonder sometimes if we need the opposite to agile. We need sudden leaps and then stability. We need huge new paradigms of systems designed in tandem with each other and forging new ground. We need continual 5 year plans. Fix, then develop, then fix again. Knowing when to stick, when to change, when to be bold, when to be fast, when to be slow, these seem more important than this sense of directionless panic and constant refinement.

"How do I persuade my company to take a more outcome-focused, less feature-factory-like approach?" 10 Ideas:1/10: Be a broken record about the goal.You'll have to build momentum behind The Why. Remember, specs and "visuals" get people excited. Vague product bets do not. Momentum is all about repetition and a crystal-clear vision. Describe the customer outcomes you are shooting for and why they will matter. 2/10: Have a high-level plan to manage risk and increase the probability of a good result.Remember, you are spending the company's money and the money/energy of your co-workers, assuming they have some stock in the company. You must devise a reasonable "game" that limits risk and increases the chance of a favorable upside. If you don't offer one, don't be surprised if someone in finance proposes one. This isn't about "creating trust." This is about being disciplined about spending money and time (especially). Opportunity cost—what you could be spending time on—is almost always your biggest concern.3/10: Detail the pivot-or-proceed points.A good game has a way to cut your losses and move on. When will you decide whether the current direction is viable or needs to change? Which signals will you monitor? What are the thresholds? What is your general approach to prioritizing experiments and deciding when to move on?4/10: Timebox everything. Timeboxes are the best form of HEALTHY forcing function. Of course, teams also misuse timeboxes all the time. But when used the right way—as a trigger to reassess what is happening, integrate the various strands of the effort, and make sure you are on course—they are very valuable. Consider a set of nested timeboxes (e.g., 2w, 6w, 12w), each cycle meant to trigger reflection.5/10: Outline your learning goals. Detail risks. Treat your efforts to learn as a product itself, and prioritize areas of uncertainty. Teams often keep their research and discovery efforts opaque, which leaves stakeholders wondering what is happening. Roadmap it out! Prioritize risk areas, and detail a plan to keep those risks in check. You'll notice the theme here: don't just shrug and say, "yeah, we'll figure it out!" Be disciplined about your approach.6/10: List representative options. Often, the key to getting buy-in for an open-ended initiative is proving you're at least capable of brainstorming some representative options. People want to know the limits of the solution space. Brainstorm enough opportunities to prevent anyone from falling in love with any particular approach. 7/10: Explain when and how you will involve other teams. At some point, other groups (e.g., marketing and customer success) will need a clearer sense of what capabilities you are delivering. You can't leave them in the dark. When you leave them hanging, they push teams to converge earlier. When you keep the lines of communication open, they'll encourage your efforts....continued in comments

Users don't care about your velocity. They couldn't care less about how many story points you deliver per Sprint 😬 When your energy goes to vanity metrics, you get meaningless results. I used to focus on increasing velocity. Once, we went from 30 story points to 72 in just six Sprints. Guess what? Nothing ever changed in our desired business outcome. 😓 We got distracted by pointless numbers and missed meaningful opportunities to create value sooner. ❌ More story points have no relation to creating more value. ❌ More live features cannot guarantee to improve your customers' lives. I decided to ditch velocity because I perceived it as a distraction instead of being helpful. I believe it's more valuable to put features live faster for a smaller audience and learn from them instead of pursuing vanity metrics. You may still opt to use velocity. Many teams still do. Yet, do you know why they use it? How does that contribute to creating more value sooner? It's not because many teams use velocity that you should use it. First, understand the why behind the how. Then, you can make a better-informed decision. Curious to hear your take on teams' velocity. #productmanagement #agile #productowner #productmanager #scrum #leadership

Here's a naughty lil secret about discovery. Many of us were taught that the purpose of discovery is to understand the customer's needs, so that we can map our solution back to those needs and show how we can help. Here's the fatal flaw in that theory. If we're asking our customers to tell us their needs, chances are, they're telling our competitors the same thing. So, the odds that our solution will be meaningfully different than our competitors are slim. As a result, customers commoditize us around the one area they CAN tell a difference. Price. How could we approach discovery instead? Here is my 5-step process:1 - Build a hypothesis on what the company trying to achieve, and seek to understand what you're missing as an outsider.*Find this in CEO letter to shareholders, interviews with the Founder, CEO guest appearances on podcasts, etc.. Bring it to the call, and frame it as, "This is what I can observe from the outside - what have I missed?."2 - Be curious about HOW that stakeholder currently believes/assumes they will accomplish that goal, and ask HOW they formed that opinion. *Example: Company's goal is to 4x subscription revenue by 2025. To do so, CRO believes they must hire more SDRs and raise activity targets. CRO believes these are the right needs, because this approach worked at his/her last 3 companies.3 - Introduce evidence that contradicts those beliefs/assumptions. *This is your reframe. Our goal isn't to tell them they're wrong. It's to introduce an enemy to that belief/assumption that breaks it. Key is to make it so you aren't attacking the person, but rather, exposing them to something environmental that makes 1+1 = 3, not 2. 4 - Give them a formula to calculate the implications of continuing with their current approach. *This is NOT an ROI formula. That's what happens IF they change. This is about COI (cost of inaction). What happens if they don't change? 5 - If you've piqued their curiosity, suggest that they collect the inputs needed to calculate the size of the pain, and bring those to the next call. Don't assemble or sell the solution yet. Just agree that we'll explore the size of the pain together, to see if this is even an area worth exploring. Discovery shouldn't feel like death by 1000 questions, or a qualification exercise. It should feel like two colleagues exploring a problem together.

What would KPI’s for life look like?KPI’s are a great way for companies to focus on what matters and progress, but we don’t tend to use them for life.In some ways you could argue the most vital KPI’s for life would focus on what most people would consider the ultimate goal, happiness or fulfillment or joy or something like that. A sort of mission or “vision” of sorts.You can't really find KPI's for these things, so we need to find there elements that lead to this. These are perhaps strategies or approaches to enable us to feel this way. What makes us happy? Yikes. What makes us fulfilled? I’m no expert on things like this, but I guess it would come down to things like a sense of purpose, health, connection to others, financial and physical security, well balanced kids with opportunities, freedom, sense of self determinism, stuff like this.Again we can’t really measure these things very accurately or rationally. Is freedom measured by saving towards FU money? Do we count number of friendships, or depth? Do we measure progress in bringing up kids by exam results? I hope not. One of the curious things about the modern world is literally everything that really and profoundly MATTERS is IMPOSSIBLE to measure. And ALL of the things that are really EASY to measure are generally meaningless. We can precisely see how many “friends” we have on Facebook or how many likes we have, but both mean little. We can of course see our precise salaries, or how many times we go to the gym or how many sq ft our home is, but these are for many people clumsy indicators. A better commute, more supportive boss, more control over our time, nicer neighbors etc may have more impact on joy. Unable to deal with the ambiguity of the metrics that matter, we tend to find proxies for them or find things that at least correlate.Owning expensive cars seems somewhat connected to success.Freedom can’t be measured, but a high salary acts as a decent proxy for it. Kids going to a good school acts as a decent indicator that they are doing to do well in life. Most people are terrible at chasing unmeasurable goals. To pursue freedom or connection is so vague as to be useless. But people are incredibly at chasing measurable outcomes or processes.We're amazing at going to the gym 3 times a week for an hour, or getting the 6 figure salary by the age of X, or getting into a specific tranche of university.The problem is when these proxies override the ultimate aims. When chasing promotions makes you deeply miserable and anxious, where owning a home with a garden becomes a blinder to your sense of purpose. Somehow we've lost the end goals for the proxies we could measure. I do business advice, not personal advice, but we do the same at work. We focus on the measurable proxies, on the quantifiable aspects of a process, we focus on things that have no meaning, because the things that really matter are just felt.I wonder what could be done about it.

Kickoff calls need an overhaul.Everyone hops on a Zoom - its a mix of strategic and tactical items and rarely is anyone paying attention. Count how many times you catch eyes moving to a second screen....And who said it has to be this way? Make a change.The old way:- 1 hour long- stale ice breakers- mixing strategy & tactics- asking the customer to repeat themselvesThe new way:(Pre-meeting)- asking the economic buyer/champion to build a one-page memo on why they bought the solution, the strategy, and where it fits- send a 5-10min video summary out to the group showcasing the memo(during the actual meeting)- invite those who can contribute to the timeline- ask for an ice breaker that can bring you closer and build an emotional connection- focus on spending your time with the tactics on how to implement and execute- build a follow-up one-page memo of decisions that were made and progress forwardWe must stop monopolizing time because "that's the way its always been done"(And if they can't build a 1 page memo on why they bought your solution, then I'd put them at risk to begin with)Forget valuable, make it interesting…make it something that really is unique of an experience they've had before. THEN they will *pull* you into their business.Don’t treat a kickoff as a check the box. Make it worthwhile.How are you adapting your kick-off calls to increase attendance and engagement?—#customersuccess#saas#marketing

Universities are supposed to be bastions of intelligence and critical thinking. But the Covid policies they have adopted are anti-scientific and often downright cruel. In this article, I share the findings of my reporting on some of America’s most prestigious universities. And they were jaw-dropping:Campuses are instituting martial law to transiently delay a mild infection among America's lowest risk population. This is nothing short of an abuse of power on a defenseless population. For this piece, I interviewed students (all vaccinated) subject to testing twice-a-week. One developed a nosebleed from excessive nasal swabbing. Another quit school altogether in disgust. At Georgetown, students told me how staff patrolled the library during finals week and would yell at anyone who loosens their mask to take a drink of water. Looking for a Job? Sell Your Destiny, Not Your History
Bruce Kasanoff
Bruce Kasanoff 
Creative Partner — call me when you're stuck just short of a breakthrough


August 27, 2018
It's a simple question, and you've probably answered it hundreds of times. "What do you do?" If you're like most people, you probably get the answer dead wrong.

Your standard reply is probably a factual description of your current job.

The right answer is: what you want to do.

Most job candidates get so caught up in retelling the past that they fail to paint a compelling picture of the shared future they (you) could co-create with a new employer.

Let's break down the end of that last sentence.

Paint a compelling picture: If good is the enemy of great, then boredom is the enemy of getting hired. Hiring managers have to slog through countless interviews with people who drone on and on about things that don't interest the manager. Interview 10 people and I guarantee you'll have a hard time remembering what at least six of them said.

Before you do anything else, you need to engage others. That means being interesting and memorable. Jerry Seinfeld spent over a year crafting a new stand-up comedy routine after his TV show ended; essentially, he was preparing to get a new job. If the most successful comedian in the world needs to invest that much time and effort, what makes you think that simply reciting your previous "shows" will be sufficient? Would you be satisfied if Jerry simply got up on stage and said, "I played Philadelphia on July 12, 2005. Then I played Detroit on July 15, 2005. Then . . ."?

Of the shared future: Let's consider what's actually happening when you are "job hunting." You and your potential employer have been spending 99.9999% of your time apart, pursuing separate interests and objectives. Suddenly, you will be spending half your time together. What will that look like? Can you describe it vividly and in a highly compelling manner?

Most people can't for two reasons.

The first is that you haven't done your homework. You don't know much about the organization or people with whom you are talking. Not just their sales, but also their culture, customers, habits, challenges, opportunities and quirks. Especially their quirks. So do your homework, even though it takes a ton of time.

The second reason you aren't painting a compelling story around your potential is that you are in the habit of talking about your history, rather than your destiny.

Sure, I care that you know how to code and that you worked in the oil and gas industry. But have you always dreamed about building something big, something into which you could pour your heart and soul, something so important that nothing—absolutely nothing and no one—will stand in your way? Say that.

You could co-create with a new employer: Our world is far too complicated to go it alone. This is as true for Apple as it is for you. When talented and perceptive people pursue new opportunities, they understand the true nature of what is happening: together, you are exploring what you could co-create.

Exploring co-creation is nothing like interviewing for a job. In a co-creation conversation, you move quickly past your history and excitedly start to explore possibilities. Together, you start to envision an idealized future. Together, you recognize each others' strengths and weaknesses; it dawns on all of you that together you have a brighter future than any of you do apart.

This, by the way, is why some people with basically the same qualifications earn $1 million a year while others earn $100,000.

If you want to elevate and accelerate your career, don't just interview. Co-create.

Bruce Kasanoff is the co-founder of Park City Think Tank, which serves as a thinking partner for executives and innovators. We help you engage and inspire the people that matter most to you.

This post was part of Forbes’ Career Challenge: Get Job-Search Ready In 15 Days.

How To Write A Cover Letter
James Caan CBE
James Caan CBE 
Recruitment Entrepreneur Chairman | Serial Entrepreneur | Investor on BBC's Dragons’ Den (2007-2010)


September 9, 2016
In my last blog I shared some of my best CV tips, things to avoid, and how I prefer to see a CV structured. 

This week, it’s time to master the cover letter.

Firstly, if you’re ever deliberating whether writing a cover letter is actually worth it, because you think employers automatically disregard anything they need to spend more than two minutes reading, you’re wrong, and that’s probably why you didn’t land that interview you really wanted.

I touched on the difference between a CV and cover letter last week; your CV is full of fact, it details very clearly the experience you have that will enable you to add value to the role.

Your cover letter, on the other hand, is where you add the colour surrounding that experience. This is where you display your personality, and show the employer you’re more than a bunch of grades and work experience. 

Similarly to your CV, a cover letter needs to very specifically address the job description you’ve been given. It helps to take a copy of the description, and highlight the employers key candidate criteria.

However, whereas your CV needs to be very concise and to the point, the cover letter is your opportunity to include all the stuff you wish you could include within your CV, the content you reluctantly delete to ensure its not too long.

So, here’s an example of what to include:

Who, what, why 

Open the letter by clearly stating who you are, what you do, and why you’re applying for the role. For example, if you’re John from accounts applying for as role as Head of Accounts, you could say;

“As an established senior accountant with over 10 years experience in the financial sector, I believe I possess the expertise, market knowledge, passion and agility to excel as Head of Accounts within your organisation.”

What you’ve achieved and why you’re suitable

Next it’s time to add colour to your experience. This is the body of your letter. It needs be engaging, and direct - you really need to sell yourself. Don’t feel embarrassed to let the reader know where you excel, your personal achievements and what you’re really great at. 

Your highlighted criteria from the job description will lead your structure here. It’s important you link your experience with what the employer is looking for. For example, if i wanted a Head of Accounts to manage a small team of three, John could include something like;

“One of the main objectives in my current role is mentoring my colleagues and ensuring that, as their manager, I understand how I can support and ensure their productivity levels are consistently improving. To do this, I ask for weekly reporting slides, organise regular catchup meetings and employ an open door policy, so they feel comfortable asking me for advice at any time.”

Reading this, I instantly know this candidate has the required managerial experience, so he’s already impressing me. 

The future

It makes sense to end your cover letter with ambitions for the future. Here, i’d reiterate why this particular role excites you and why you’re worthy of an interview. 

Let them know you’re looking forward to hearing more about the opportunity, and leave the relevant contact details. 

Once you’ve sent the cover letter, it’s always a good idea to follow up with a phone call a week later. 

Good luck!

Founders, here’s something I learned the hard way:

The TAM-SOM Paradox:

We often hear about needing a huge market (TAM) to attract VCs.

Yet, when you're in the trenches, your world revolves around SOM. Every move, every decision, it's about being lean and effective.

To succeed, you must start uncomfortably narrow.

But VCs are eyeing the TAM.

I've seen many founders, including myself, grapple with this.

Start too broad, and you’ll scatter your resources too thin.
Fail to communicate your TAM, and you’ll lose top-tier investors.

It's a paradox, but it's also the key to success.

So, my advice?

Perfect your SOM, but plan for TAM.
Show VCs not just where you are, but where you could be.

You don't need to compromise on vision for execution.

Focus on your SOM for efficiency.

But never lose sight of your TAM for growth potential.

5 ways to encourage use of AI within your company .... especially if you're a little freaked out about giving up control and letting teams run wild. 

1. Train the full team on what is and isn't possible with AI - in the real world, not the fake 'OMG I just laid off 8 sales reps and now I have an agent running my $5M company' world. 

AI isn't magic. It follows the same data standards of previous technologies: garbage in, garbage out. In the old days, if you rolled out Tableau with access to all your company data, you'd probably run some big trainings on how to use it properly. The AI age is no different. 

2. Create a couple of apps or assistants as inspiration for the team. Tie them to company data, company workflow, etc. Tell your team EXACTLY how to use them. "Ask these questions but don't ask these questions." Help them understand what the AI is expertly tuned for and what it isn't. (This is why open-ended chatbots kind of freak me out.) 

3. Create a public Slackbot for people to query these assistants. Leave it in public so you can creep on it and see what people are asking and if they're querying the data "properly" 

4. Set expectations (and policies) on what can be used internally vs externally. You might need to be a pain in the ass here. When you're given data, ask "how'd you get the answer?" Find out if people used AI and - if they did - question the methodology. Better to be safe than sorry. Don't want any data shared with a client or external stakeholder without it going through a review? State that up front. 

5. Assign one person internally to be a true subject matter expert. Make their time available to coach and support other teams. There are a lot of people at your company that are curious and intrigued .... but maybe also a little scared or self-conscious. Given them a peer resource to support them.

Most PM work is therapy, not Jira.
Frameworks? You can learn them anywhere.
Prioritisation? Google will happily explain it.
Roadmaps? There’s a template for every flavour.

But the part no one really prepares you for is this:
- Navigating egos.
- Defusing tension before it turns into conflict.
- Spotting fear when it’s disguised as urgency.
- Getting people aligned when everyone swears they already are.
- Turning a room full of chaos into something that feels calm and possible.

These aren’t “nice-to-haves.” They are the job.
If you want to become a stronger PM, study humans.
How they think, how they react, what they’re afraid of, what motivates them.
The tools will follow.

This Shopify update is a preview of how every digital business will operate in the next few years.

Shopify’s Winter ‘26 “RenAIssance Edition” isn’t just another release. It highlights a shift already underway across digital products, not just e-commerce.

There are more than 150 updates, but three ideas show where things are heading.

SimGym
AI shoppers that behave like real customers. They browse, hesitate, abandon, and complete purchases.
Merchants can now test changes without waiting for real traffic.
Synthetic customers produce useful signals in hours instead of long cycles.
And this won’t stay in commerce. Any digital experience with enough behavioural history will eventually enable synthetic testing.

Agentic Storefronts
Products can now be discovered inside ChatGPT, Perplexity, and Copilot.
One setup, no bespoke integrations.
Discovery and transaction are starting to happen inside conversations, not on websites.
Shopify’s approach is simple: treat AI platforms as distribution, not competition. If customers are making decisions inside conversations, meet them there.

Sidekick
Moving from assistant to operator.
It spots friction, suggests improvements, builds small tools from plain language, and carries out changes with merchant approval.

The pattern is becoming clearer:

→ Faster experimentation
→ Reduced reliance on traffic
→ Discovery shifting to AI environments
→ Websites becoming one of many touchpoints rather than the centre of the experience

And there’s a deeper implication most companies haven’t confronted yet.

When discovery and purchases happen inside AI conversations, your traditional structures stop working:
your cookie banner never fires, attribution becomes unreliable, and consent models start to break.
Shopify is preparing for a world where customers may not touch your site at all.

But the most important point sits underneath everything.

SimGym works because Shopify holds years of behavioural data across millions of stores.
Browsing patterns, checkout decisions, drop-off signals, micro-behaviours.
That context is the engine behind these capabilities.

The models powering this are widely available.
The behavioural history is not.

This is the emerging reality across every sector.
Your advantage won’t come from using the same AI models as everyone else. It will come from the behavioural and operational context you’ve built over time. Without that, you’re renting capability rather than strengthening it.

A useful question for any leadership team right now:

What behavioural or operational data do we already hold that could power the next generation of our products or services?

The companies that can answer this clearly will move quicker than those relying solely on generic tools.

In enterprise RAG, “retrieved from XYZ.pdf” is NOT enough.
Legal & compliance teams want precise provenance — clause, page, section, and even bounding boxes.

Here’s how real teams build traceable RAG:

⸻

1️⃣ Store rich metadata at ingestion

Every chunk must store:
	•	document ID
	•	section/clause ID
	•	page number
	•	PDF bounding box
	•	version ID + timestamps

This ensures every chunk points back to the exact source location.

⸻

2️⃣ Retrieval must return metadata, not just text

Retriever output =
{chunk_text, doc_id, section_id, page_no, coords}
This metadata flows end-to-end through the system.

⸻

3️⃣ Log what was actually used

Your pipeline should log:
	•	which chunks were retrieved
	•	which ones were fed into the model
	•	which ones were cited in the final answer

Perfect for audits.

⸻

4️⃣ UI-level inline citations

Display answers like:
“…per policy [Doc 12, clause 4.3]”
Tapping it expands to the exact paragraph/page.

This removes ambiguity for legal teams.

⸻

5️⃣ Use Traceability Tools (optional but powerful)

Teams often plug in:
	•	Arize AI → monitors retrieved chunks vs. generated answer
	•	TruLens → faithfulness, citations, trace graphs
	•	WhyLabs → data + retrieval drift monitoring
	•	LlamaIndex Observability → end-to-end provenance tracing

These tools generate trace graphs showing EXACT which chunk impacted each sentence.

⸻

6️⃣ Full audit trail

Store everything per query:
	•	user input
	•	retrieved chunks & metadata
	•	model output
	•	cited source locations

This is mandatory for regulated domains.

⸻

⭐ Why it matters

This is how enterprise RAG becomes:
✔ transparent
✔ defensible
✔ audit-ready
✔ safe for legal, compliance & enterprise workloads

The 5 Rules for High-Converting Content:
1. It is easier to go from winning on X to winning on IG than the other way around. Trends start on X. People there are having higher-level conversations. In my opinion, content trends flow in this order: X → TikTok → Reddit → YouTube → Instagram.

If you are seeing a trend in multiple places on IG, you are already too late.

2. Stop doing "You are doing X, Y, and Z wrong" hooks. Start sharing "Here is what I’d do" instead. People are sick of being attacked on IG. (e.g., "YOU FEEL BRAIN FOG AND LAZINESS BECAUSE OF THIS ONE THING").

People are tired of being talked at. I told ECA two months ago to stop doing "you-based" content and instead rewrite all hooks into one of these three formats:

"If I was [insert nightmare your avatar lives in], here is exactly what I’d do."

"Here are my first 3 moves if I had [specific problem they have]."

"[Client Name] came in with [mess]. Here is the step-by-step of how we fixed it."

Go back through your last 20 IG posts. If anything starts with a "you" attack, remake it following this framework. The metric of being heard and understood by your ideal client will undoubtedly be greater.

3. Build content from real client data. Every post should come from proof: check-ins, DM screenshots, sales calls, community questions, etc. This is the real stuff people are coming to you for.

The Play: Place a cropped screenshot of a client question in a Reel or Green Screen and answer it.

The Benefit: This is an amazing opportunity to plug in social proof.

For each question, answer with a step-by-step: "What did I actually do to fix this?" This creates an endless content vault that showcases you as the authority on the problems you constantly solve.

4. Make the content so simple a fifth-grader could execute it. You likely overcomplicate things by accident to flex your knowledge; I have definitely done that in the past. However, complexity breaks conversions.

See how simple this 5-step sauce from yesterday's ECA content call is to understand? You probably suck at content, and you get it. Your ideal client sucks at fixing the problem you fix for them—so meet them where they are at. Use a fifth-grade reading level.

Tip: Use ChatGPT to help you simplify your content before you speak it or post it.

5. Every piece of content must have a next-step item. Where do they go? What do they do now?

This doesn't have to be a "Hard CTA" asking them to inquire with you. A "Soft CTA" asking them to track their body temps for 3 days and then DM you the results is great. This gives you the opportunity to help them in the DMs to see if they have an issue that you solve.

Content is sales. Not just sales in terms of dollars, but sales in terms of selling your ideology to find people who align with it and disqualifying those who don't.

Summary According to my highly trained GPT, these are the five pieces of "content sauce" you keep screaming into the void in your 1:1 operator calls:

Early trend hunting

Better hooks

Client-data-driven topics

Brutal simplicity

Pipeline-first content
